ch01.title: What is Machine Learning?
ch01.summary: ML is the field where computers learn to perform tasks from experience without being explicitly programmed. Tom Mitchell's definition ties together task, performance, and experience into a formal framework that covers everything from spam filters to self-driving cars.

sections.heading: Core Definition (Mitchell 1997)
sections.text: A computer program is said to learn from experience $E$ with respect to some class of tasks $T$ and performance measure $P$, if its performance at tasks in $T$, as measured by $P$, improves with experience $E$. This is deliberately abstract: $T$ could be classifying emails, $E$ could be labeled spam/not-spam examples, and $P$ could be classification accuracy. The key insight is that the program gets better automatically вҖ” it is not just executing fixed rules but updating its internal model based on data.

sections.heading: The Learning Problem Formulation
sections.text: Every ML problem reduces to learning a function $y = f(x; w)$ where $x$ is the input feature vector, $y$ is the output, and $w$ are the learnable parameters. Classification: $y$ is a discrete label from $\{C_1,\ldots,C_K\}$ вҖ” e.g. spam/not-spam. Regression: $y \in \mathbb{R}$ вҖ” e.g. predicting house prices. The learning algorithm searches for the parameters $w^*$ that minimise some loss function over a training set. Crucially, we want $w^*$ to generalise вҖ” to work on new, unseen inputs, not just memorise the training data.

sections.heading: Unified Formulation
sections.text:
Classification: $y = f(x;w),\quad y \in \{C_1, \ldots, C_K\}$
Regression: $y = f(x;w),\quad y \in \mathbb{R}$

Learning objective:
$$w^* = \arg\min_w\; \mathbb{E}_{(x,y)\sim p_{\text{data}}}\bigl[\mathcal{L}(y,\, f(x;w))\bigr]$$

In practice we minimise over the training set (empirical risk minimisation).

sections.heading: Why not just program the rules?
sections.text: For face recognition: a human programmer cannot enumerate rules for all possible faces under all lighting conditions, poses, and expressions. But showing 1 million labeled face images to a learning algorithm produces a model that generalises. The knowledge is encoded implicitly in the parameters вҖ” ML extracts structure from data that is too complex to specify manually. This is what makes ML transformative: it scales with data, not with human effort.

ch01.title: Types of Learning
ch01.summary: ML algorithms differ fundamentally by the type of supervision they receive. The choice of paradigm determines what kind of data is needed, what problems can be solved, and how performance is measured вҖ” understanding this taxonomy is essential before picking an algorithm.

sections.heading: Supervised Learning вҖ” The Course Focus
sections.text: In supervised learning, each training example consists of an input $x_n$ and a corresponding target label $t_n$. The algorithm learns a mapping from inputs to outputs by minimising a loss function that measures discrepancy between predictions $f(x_n; w)$ and targets $t_n$. The majority of this course вҖ” Bayes decision theory, linear discriminants, SVMs, neural networks, CNNs, RNNs, Transformers вҖ” is about supervised learning. The underlying mathematical structure is always: define a model, define a loss, optimise.

left.title: Supervised
points: Needs labeled data вҖ” expensive to collect
Risk of learning shortcuts in labels

right.title: Unsupervised
points: No labels needed вҖ” unlimited data

ch01.title: Probability Theory Essentials
ch01.summary: Probability theory is the mathematical language of ML. The sum rule, product rule, and Bayes theorem are not just formulas вҖ” they are the fundamental operations for reasoning under uncertainty, and underlie everything from Bayes Decision Theory to variational autoencoders.

sections.heading: The Two Rules of Probability
sections.text:
Sum rule (marginalisation вҖ” integrate out a variable):
$$p(X) = \sum_Y p(X,Y) \qquad \text{(discrete)}$$

Product rule (chain rule вҖ” factorise a joint distribution):
$$p(X,Y) = p(Y\mid X)\cdot p(X) = p(X\mid Y)\cdot p(Y)$$

Bayes theorem (derived by dividing both factorizations):
$$\boxed{p(Y\mid X) = \frac{p(X\mid Y)\cdot p(Y)}{p(X)}}$$

sections.heading: Why These Rules Are Everything
sections.text: These two rules are complete: any probabilistic computation can be reduced to repeated application of sum and product rules. The sum rule lets us marginalise (remove) unwanted variables вҖ” essential for computing posteriors over classes when we have a joint model. The product rule lets us factorise complex joint distributions into manageable pieces вҖ” this is the foundation of graphical models, naive Bayes, and Bayesian networks. Bayes theorem is simply the product rule applied twice and divided: it converts $p(\text{evidence}|\text{hypothesis})$ into $p(\text{hypothesis}|\text{evidence})$, which is the direction we actually need for inference.

sections.heading: Key Quantities for Classifiers
sections.text:
Prior:      $p(C_k)$       вҖ” frequency of class $k$ before seeing $x$
Likelihood: $p(x\mid C_k)$ вҖ” probability of observation $x$ under class $k$
Posterior:  $p(C_k\mid x)$ вҖ” probability of class $k$ after observing $x$
Evidence:   $p(x) = \sum_k p(x\mid C_k)\,p(C_k)$ вҖ” normalising constant

$$p(C_k\mid x) = \frac{p(x\mid C_k)\cdot p(C_k)}{p(x)}$$

sections.heading: Expectations, Variance, Covariance
sections.text: The expectation $\mathbb{E}[f(x)] = \sum_x p(x)f(x)$ is a weighted average of $f$ вҖ” the 'centre of mass' of the distribution. The variance $\mathrm{Var}[x] = \mathbb{E}[x^2] - (\mathbb{E}[x])^2$ measures spread around the mean. For vectors, the covariance matrix $\Sigma_{ij} = \mathbb{E}[(x_i - \mu_i)(x_j - \mu_j)]$ captures how dimensions co-vary. These are the sufficient statistics for Gaussian distributions, which appear throughout this course. The covariance matrix is also central to PCA, LDA, and the Mahalanobis distance used in Bayes classifiers.

ch01.title: Model Complexity and Generalisation
ch01.summary: Training error alone is a useless metric вҖ” what matters is performance on unseen data (generalisation). The bias-variance tradeoff explains why both too-simple and too-complex models fail, and regularisation is the principled way to navigate between them.

sections.heading: The Core Problem: Overfitting vs Underfitting
sections.text: A polynomial of degree $N-1$ can perfectly interpolate $N$ training points, achieving zero training error. But it may oscillate wildly between the training points, giving catastrophic test error. This is overfitting: the model has memorised the training data including its noise, rather than learning the underlying pattern. The opposite extreme is underfitting: a model too simple to capture the structure of the data, giving high error on both training and test sets. Almost all of ML is about finding the right balance.

left.title: Underfitting (High Bias)
points: Model too simple for the true pattern
High training error AND high test error
Strong prior assumptions constrain the model
The error cannot be reduced by adding more data
Fix: increase model capacity, reduce regularisation

right.title: Overfitting (High Variance)
points: Low training error, high test error
Model is sensitive to small changes in training set
Adding more data typically reduces overfitting

sections.heading: Regularisation
sections.text:
Total objective with L2 regularisation (weight decay):
$$E_{\text{total}}(w) = \underbrace{E_{\text{data}}(w)}_{\text{fit training data}} + \underbrace{\frac{\lambda}{2}\|w\|^2}_{\text{penalise complexity}}$$

Large $\lambda$: forces $w \approx 0$ вҶ’ simpler model (bias вҶ‘, variance вҶ“)
Small $\lambda$: unconstrained $w$ вҶ’ complex model (bias вҶ“, variance вҶ‘)
$\lambda$ chosen by cross-validation on a held-out validation set.

sections.heading: Cross-Validation and the Test Set
sections.text: The training set is used to fit model parameters. The validation set is used to choose hyperparameters (like $\lambda$, model complexity, learning rate). The test set is used exactly once at the end to estimate true generalisation performance. Mixing these roles leads to optimistic estimates of performance. K-fold cross-validation splits data into K folds and rotates the validation set, giving a more reliable estimate when data is limited. This discipline of separating train/validation/test is essential in practice and the reason ML evaluations are credible.

ch01.title: Course Roadmap
ch01.summary: The course builds from theoretical foundations (probability, decision theory) through classical ML (Bayes, discriminants, SVMs, boosting) to modern deep learning (NNs, CNNs, RNNs, Transformers). Understanding the classical methods is essential вҖ” deep learning didn't replace them, it extended them.

sections.heading: Chapter Progression
items: Ch01 Introduction вҖ” ML definition, taxonomy, probability review, bias-variance tradeoff
Ch02 Bayes Decision Theory вҖ” optimal classifiers assuming known distributions; loss functions, three approaches
Ch03 Parametric Density Estimation вҖ” Gaussian models, MLE, MAP, EM algorithm for GMMs
Ch04 Linear Discriminants вҖ” discriminant functions, least squares, Fisher LDA, logistic regression
Ch05 SVMs вҖ” maximum margin, KKT conditions, soft margin, kernel trick
Ch06 AdaBoost вҖ” bagging, boosting, exponential loss, weak learners
Ch07 Neural Networks вҖ” MLP, backpropagation, activations, universal approximation
Ch08 Optimization & Training вҖ” SGD, Adam, batch norm, dropout, initialisation
Ch09 CNNs вҖ” convolution, pooling, architectures (ResNet), object detection
Ch10 Word Embeddings вҖ” Word2Vec (CBOW/Skip-gram), GloVe, distributional semantics
Ch11 RNNs вҖ” BPTT, vanishing gradients, LSTM, GRU, seq2seq, attention
Ch12 Transformers вҖ” self-attention, multi-head, BERT, GPT, scaling laws

sections.heading: The Big Arc: Classical to Deep
sections.text: The first half (ch02вҖ“ch06) covers classical ML with exact or convex objectives вҖ” methods we can fully analyse mathematically, prove convergence for, and understand theoretically. The second half (ch07вҖ“ch12) covers deep learning: non-convex, empirically powerful, and theoretically less understood. The connecting thread is that everything is learning a function $y = f(x; w)$ by minimising a loss вҖ” only the form of $f$ changes. Understanding logistic regression (ch04) makes softmax in neural networks obvious. Understanding SVM margin (ch05) informs why weight decay regularisation works. Classical methods are not obsolete; they are the conceptual foundation.

ch02.title: Prior, Likelihood, and Posterior
ch02.summary: Bayes Decision Theory is the gold standard for classification: given perfect knowledge of distributions, it tells us the theoretically optimal decision rule. In practice we never have this perfect knowledge, but the theory tells us what every classifier is trying to approximate.
ch02.context: Lecture 2 В· 16.10.2023 вҖ” The probabilistic foundation of classification

sections.heading: Three Core Quantities
sections.text: Before observing any data, we have a prior $p(C_k)$ вҖ” the base rate of each class in the population (e.g. 0.1% of emails are spam). When we observe input $x$, the class-conditional likelihood $p(x\mid C_k)$ tells us how probable $x$ would be if we were in class $k$ (e.g. how likely is this word pattern given it's spam?). Applying Bayes theorem converts these into the posterior $p(C_k\mid x)$: the probability of each class given the specific observation $x$ we actually received. The posterior is the only quantity we need for making decisions, because it incorporates both the evidence ($x$) and the base rate ($p(C_k)$).

sections.heading: Bayes Theorem for Classification
sections.text:
$$\underbrace{p(C_k\mid x)}_{\text{posterior}} = \frac{\overbrace{p(x\mid C_k)}^{\text{likelihood}}\cdot\overbrace{p(C_k)}^{\text{prior}}}{\underbrace{p(x)}_{\text{evidence}}}$$

$p(x) = \sum_k p(x\mid C_k)\,p(C_k)$ вҖ” just a normalising constant

Optimal decision (minimises misclassification rate):
$$k^* = \arg\max_k\; p(C_k\mid x)$$

sections.heading: Likelihood-Ratio Test (2 classes)
sections.text:
Decide $C_1$ if posterior ratio $> 1$:
$$\frac{p(C_1\mid x)}{p(C_2\mid x)} > 1 \iff \frac{p(x\mid C_1)}{p(x\mid C_2)} > \frac{p(C_2)}{p(C_1)}$$

The right-hand side is the ratio of priors. If $C_2$ is 10Г— more common, you need 10Г— stronger evidence for $C_1$ to overcome the base rate. This is the mathematically correct way to account for class imbalance.

sections.heading: Why the posterior, not the likelihood?
sections.text: A common mistake is to classify based on the highest likelihood $p(x|C_k)$. But a very typical-looking $x$ under class $k$ doesn't mean class $k$ is the right answer if class $k$ is very rare. A disease test that's 99% sensitive but the disease affects 0.1% of people: even a positive test gives only ~9% posterior probability of having the disease (the famous base-rate neglect error). The posterior automatically corrects for this.

ch02.title: Loss Functions and the Reject Option
ch02.summary: Simply minimising misclassification rate is not always the right objective. In medical diagnosis, misclassifying a sick patient as healthy is far more costly than the reverse. A general loss matrix captures asymmetric costs, leading to decision rules that shift the boundary toward the more dangerous error.
ch02.context: Lecture 2 В· 16.10.2023 вҖ” Optimal decision making under loss

sections.heading: Loss Matrix
sections.text: Define $L_{kj}$ as the loss incurred when we predict class $C_j$ but the true class is $C_k$. The standard 0-1 loss has $L_{kj} = 1 - \delta_{kj}$ (all mistakes equally bad). But in cancer diagnosis: missing a malignant tumour ($L_{\text{cancer},\text{healthy}}$) may be 100Г— worse than a false alarm ($L_{\text{healthy},\text{cancer}}$). In fraud detection: letting fraud through costs more than blocking a legitimate transaction. The loss matrix encodes the domain-specific cost structure that the decision rule should optimise.

sections.heading: Expected Loss Minimisation
sections.text:
Expected risk of deciding $C_j$ given observation $x$:
$$R(C_j\mid x) = \sum_k L_{kj}\cdot p(C_k\mid x)$$

Optimal decision (minimises expected loss):
$$j^* = \arg\min_j\; R(C_j\mid x)$$

With 0-1 loss this reduces to MAP:
$$j^* = \arg\max_k\; p(C_k\mid x)$$

With asymmetric loss, the decision boundary shifts toward the more costly class.

sections.heading: The Reject Option
sections.text: Sometimes the right answer is 'I don't know'. Add a special 'reject' action with constant loss $\theta$. We reject when $\min_j R(C_j|x) > \theta$, i.e. when even the best classification decision still has too high expected loss. Equivalently, reject when $\max_k p(C_k|x) < 1-\theta$ вҖ” when we're too uncertain. Setting $\theta$ trades off error rate against rejection rate: low $\theta$ means we reject aggressively, high $\theta$ means we always commit. In self-driving cars and medical AI, building in a 'defer to human' option for uncertain cases is essential.

left.title: 0-1 Loss (symmetric)
points: Appropriate when FP and FN cost the same

right.title: Asymmetric Loss Matrix
points: Decision boundary shifts toward costly class
Essential for medical, fraud, safety applications

ch02.title: Three Approaches to Classification
ch02.summary: There are three fundamentally different ways to build a classifier, and understanding their tradeoffs is one of the most important conceptual lessons of the course. The choice affects sample efficiency, robustness, interpretability, and what the model can do beyond classification.
ch02.context: Lecture 2 В· 16.10.2023 вҖ” Connecting theory to practice

sections.heading: Generative Models вҖ” Model the Full Process
sections.text: Generative models first learn how each class 'generates' data: they fit $p(x|C_k)$ (typically a Gaussian) and estimate $p(C_k)$ from class frequencies in training data. At test time, they apply Bayes theorem. The advantage: with the correct generative model and limited data, they are more sample-efficient. They can also generate new samples, detect out-of-distribution inputs (low $p(x)$), and naturally handle missing features. The disadvantage: if the generative model is wrong (data isn't actually Gaussian), the classifier degrades. Also, modelling $p(x)$ is harder than modelling $p(C_k|x)$ вҖ” it spends capacity on the distribution of $x$ which may not help classification.

sections.heading: Discriminative vs Discriminant Functions
sections.text: Discriminative models directly fit $p(C_k|x)$, skipping $p(x)$. They only model what matters for the decision. Logistic regression is the canonical example: it models the posterior as a sigmoid applied to a linear function. Discriminant function models don't estimate probabilities at all вҖ” they learn $f_k(x)$ that ranks classes. SVMs are discriminant: they find a maximum-margin hyperplane without any probabilistic interpretation. Modern deep networks are usually discriminant (with a softmax output that produces pseudo-probabilities, but trained purely discriminatively).

sections.heading: Which approach dominates?
sections.text: With unlimited data: discriminative models generally win (they make fewer assumptions). With very limited data and correct model assumptions: generative models are more efficient. In practice: deep discriminative models trained on massive datasets dominate. But generative modelling has a renaissance in deep learning (VAEs, GANs, diffusion models) вҖ” just out of scope of this course.

ch02.title: LDA and QDA: Gaussian Class-Conditionals
ch02.summary: When class-conditional densities are Gaussian, the Bayes-optimal decision boundary can be computed analytically. Shared covariance gives a linear boundary (LDA); separate covariances give quadratic (QDA). LDA turns out to be algebraically identical to logistic regression under Gaussian assumptions.

sections.heading: Derivation Setup
sections.text: Assume $p(x|C_k) = \mathcal{N}(x;\mu_k,\Sigma_k)$. The Bayes-optimal decision rule for 2 classes is: decide $C_1$ if $\log[p(C_1|x)/p(C_2|x)] > 0$. Substituting the Gaussian densities and simplifying, the log-posterior-ratio becomes a function of $x$. The shape of this function вҖ” linear or quadratic in $x$ вҖ” depends entirely on whether the class covariances are equal.

sections.heading: LDA vs QDA Boundaries
sections.text:
QDA ($\Sigma_1 \neq \Sigma_2$) вҖ” quadratic surface:
$$x^\top(\Sigma_1^{-1}-\Sigma_2^{-1})x + \text{linear}(x) + \text{const} = 0$$

LDA ($\Sigma_1 = \Sigma_2 = \Sigma$) вҖ” quadratic terms cancel:
$$w^\top x + w_0 = 0 \qquad \text{(hyperplane)}$$
$$w = \Sigma^{-1}(\mu_1-\mu_2)$$
$$w_0 = -\tfrac{1}{2}(\mu_1+\mu_2)^\top\Sigma^{-1}(\mu_1-\mu_2) + \ln\frac{p(C_1)}{p(C_2)}$$

sections.heading: Parameter Estimation
sections.text: LDA parameters are estimated from training data using maximum likelihood: $\hat{\mu}_k = \frac{1}{N_k}\sum_{n\in C_k} x_n$ (class means), $\hat{\Sigma} = \frac{1}{N}\sum_k\sum_{n\in C_k}(x_n-\hat{\mu}_k)(x_n-\hat{\mu}_k)^\top$ (pooled covariance). QDA estimates a separate $\hat{\Sigma}_k$ per class, requiring more data. LDA needs $O(D^2)$ parameters for $\Sigma$; QDA needs $O(KD^2)$ вҖ” K times more. With 1000 features and 10 classes, QDA needs ~5M parameters vs ~500K for LDA.

sections.heading: LDA = Logistic Regression
sections.text: Remarkably, if class-conditionals are Gaussian with equal covariance, applying Bayes theorem gives exactly: $p(C_1|x) = \sigma(w^\top x + w_0)$ вҖ” the logistic sigmoid applied to a linear function. So LDA and logistic regression make identical predictions. They differ only in estimation: LDA uses MLE on each class separately (generative); logistic regression maximises the conditional likelihood $p(t|x)$ directly (discriminative). When Gaussian assumptions hold, LDA is more efficient. When they don't, logistic regression is more robust.

ch03.title: The Gaussian Distribution
ch03.summary: The Gaussian is the cornerstone of parametric density estimation. Mathematically tractable and justified by the Central Limit Theorem, it leads to closed-form solutions. The covariance matrix shape determines model capacity and required data.

sections.heading: Univariate and Multivariate Gaussian
sections.text:
Univariate:
$$\mathcal{N}(x;\mu,\sigma^2) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi\sigma^2}}\exp\!\left(-\frac{(x-\mu)^2}{2\sigma^2}\right)$$

Multivariate ($D$-dimensional):
$$\mathcal{N}(x;\mu,\Sigma) = \frac{1}{(2\pi)^{D/2}|\Sigma|^{1/2}}\exp\!\left(-\frac{1}{2}(x-\mu)^\top\Sigma^{-1}(x-\mu)\right)$$

Mahalanobis distance: $\Delta^2 = (x-\mu)^\top\Sigma^{-1}(x-\mu)$
Contours of equal density are ellipsoids centred at $\mu$.

sections.heading: Understanding the Mahalanobis Distance
sections.text: The term $(x-\mu)^\top\Sigma^{-1}(x-\mu)$ is the Mahalanobis distance вҖ” a generalisation of standard Euclidean distance that accounts for correlations between dimensions and different scales. Consider a 2D Gaussian with $\sigma_x = 10$ and $\sigma_y = 1$: a point $(5, 0)$ is only 0.5 standard deviations away in $x$, while $(0, 0.5)$ is half a standard deviation in $y$. Euclidean distance says $(5,0)$ is farther; Mahalanobis says they are equally likely. The covariance inverse $\Sigma^{-1}$ 'whitens' the space so the Gaussian becomes spherical before measuring distance. This is why LDA uses $\Sigma^{-1}(\mu_1-\mu_2)$ as the decision direction: it is the optimal direction in Mahalanobis-normalised space.

sections.heading: The Bias-Variance Trade-off in Covariance Choice
sections.text: With $D=100$ features, a full covariance matrix has $100\times101/2 = 5050$ parameters. If you only have 200 data points per class, you cannot reliably estimate 5050 numbers вҖ” the sample covariance will be singular or poorly conditioned. Restricting to diagonal gives only 100 parameters; isotropic gives just 1. This is the bias-variance trade-off applied to density estimation: fewer parameters means less accuracy in describing the true distribution (higher bias) but much more reliable estimation from limited data (lower variance). Naive Bayes assumes diagonal (features independent given class) вҖ” wrong for natural data, but surprisingly robust in high dimensions where the full covariance is unestimable anyway.

sections.heading: Why Gaussian everywhere?
sections.text: Many natural phenomena approximate Gaussian (CLT: sum of many small independent effects). More importantly, the Gaussian is the maximum entropy distribution given fixed mean and variance вҖ” it makes the fewest assumptions beyond these two moments. Any distribution with finite variance, under mild conditions, will have its sample mean converge to a Gaussian by the CLT. This means that when we model class-conditional distributions as Gaussian, we are making the weakest possible assumption consistent with knowing the mean and variance of the class. It also leads to tractable closed-form solutions: products of Gaussians are Gaussian, Gaussian marginals are Gaussian, and the Bayes-optimal boundary is analytically computable.

ch03.title: Maximum Likelihood Estimation (MLE)
ch03.summary: MLE finds the parameters that make the observed data most probable. For Gaussians, MLE gives the sample mean (unbiased) and biased sample variance вҖ” a clean result with an important subtlety about the degrees of freedom.
ch03.context: Lectures 4вҖ“5 В· 23вҖ“25.10.2023 вҖ” Fitting models to data

sections.heading: MLE Principle
sections.text:
Given i.i.d. data $\mathcal{X} = \{x_1,\ldots,x_N\}$ from $p(x\mid\theta)$:

Log-likelihood (convert product to sum):
$$\ln L(\theta) = \sum_{n=1}^N \ln p(x_n\mid\theta)$$

MLE estimate:
$$\theta_{\text{MLE}} = \arg\max_\theta \ln L(\theta)$$

sections.heading: Why Log-Likelihood?
sections.text: The likelihood $L(\theta) = \prod_n p(x_n|\theta)$ is a product over $N$ very small numbers вҖ” for continuous data, individual densities can be arbitrarily small. In practice, this product underflows to zero for even moderate $N$. Taking the log converts the product to a sum $\sum_n \ln p(x_n|\theta)$, which is numerically stable. Crucially, since $\log$ is a monotone function, $\arg\max \ln L = \arg\max L$ вҖ” the maximiser is the same. The log also cancels the exponential in Gaussian densities, turning the $\exp(-\frac{1}{2}\dots)$ into a quadratic, making differentiation straightforward.

sections.heading: MLE for the Gaussian
sections.text:
Setting $\partial\ln L / \partial\mu = 0$:
$$\mu_{\text{MLE}} = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{n=1}^N x_n \qquad \text{(UNBIASED)}$$

Setting $\partial\ln L / \partial\sigma^2 = 0$:
$$\sigma^2_{\text{MLE}} = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{n=1}^N(x_n - \mu_{\text{MLE}})^2 \qquad \text{(BIASED!)}$$

Unbiased estimator uses $\frac{1}{N-1}$ instead of $\frac{1}{N}$.

sections.heading: Why is MLE variance biased?
sections.text: The MLE variance formula uses $\hat{\mu}_{\text{MLE}}$ (estimated from the same data) rather than the true $\mu$. The sample mean $\hat{\mu}$ is constructed to minimise the sum of squared deviations from the data вҖ” it sits at the 'centre of mass'. This means it is always at least as close to the data points as the true $\mu$, so $(x_n - \hat{\mu})^2 \leq (x_n - \mu)^2$ on average. The MLE variance systematically underestimates the true variance. The bias is exactly $\sigma^2/N$ вҖ” one degree of freedom is 'used up' estimating $\mu$. With the correction $1/(N-1)$ (Bessel's correction), the estimator becomes unbiased. For large $N$ the difference is negligible; for small $N$ it matters significantly.

sections.heading: MLE for Classification
sections.text: In LDA and QDA, we use MLE to estimate class means, covariances, and prior probabilities: $\hat{p}(C_k) = N_k/N$ (fraction of training points in class $k$), $\hat{\mu}_k = \frac{1}{N_k}\sum_{n\in C_k} x_n$, and either a shared or per-class $\hat{\Sigma}$. These estimates are then plugged into the Bayes-optimal decision rule. The quality of the classifier therefore depends entirely on how well these density estimates capture the true class-conditional distributions.

ch03.title: Bayesian vs Frequentist Inference
ch03.summary: Frequentist statistics treats parameters as fixed unknowns; Bayesian statistics treats them as random variables with prior beliefs. MAP estimation is algebraically equivalent to regularised MLE вҖ” a beautiful connection between Bayesian reasoning and regularisation.
ch03.context: Lectures 4вҖ“5 В· 23вҖ“25.10.2023 вҖ” Two schools of thought

left.title: Frequentist
points: Point estimate, no uncertainty on Оё

right.title: Bayesian
points: Оё is a random variable with prior p(Оё)

sections.heading: The Frequentist View
sections.text: A frequentist thinks of probability as the long-run frequency of events in repeated experiments. The parameter $\theta$ (say, the mean of a Gaussian) is a fixed but unknown property of the world вҖ” it doesn't make sense to say 'the probability that $\mu = 3$' because $\mu$ is either 3 or it isn't. What we can say is: our estimator $\hat{\mu}$ is a random variable (because it depends on the random training data), and with 95% probability over training datasets, our confidence interval will contain the true $\mu$. MLE gives a point estimate with good statistical properties: it is consistent (converges to the true value) and asymptotically efficient (minimum variance among unbiased estimators).

sections.heading: The Bayesian View
sections.text: A Bayesian treats $\theta$ as a random variable representing our uncertainty about the world. Before seeing data, we specify a prior $p(\theta)$ that encodes our beliefs. After seeing data $\mathcal{X}$, Bayes theorem gives the posterior $p(\theta|\mathcal{X}) \propto p(\mathcal{X}|\theta)p(\theta)$. This is a full distribution over possible parameter values вҖ” richer than a point estimate. The prior is both a strength (incorporates domain knowledge, prevents nonsensical estimates with small data) and a weakness (where does the prior come from? If wrong, it biases estimates). In practice, Bayesian inference is often computationally intractable, requiring MCMC or variational approximations.

sections.heading: MAP Estimation
sections.text:
Maximum A-Posteriori вҖ” maximise the posterior:
$$\theta_{\text{MAP}} = \arg\max_\theta\; p(\theta\mid\mathcal{X}) = \arg\max_\theta\;[\ln p(\mathcal{X}\mid\theta) + \ln p(\theta)]$$

With Gaussian prior $p(\theta) = \mathcal{N}(0,\sigma_0^2)$:
$$\ln p(\theta) = -\frac{\theta^2}{2\sigma_0^2} + \text{const}$$

So MAP = MLE + L2 regularisation (with $\lambda = 1/\sigma_0^2$)!

sections.heading: MAP = Regularised MLE
sections.text: Placing a Gaussian prior on parameters and doing MAP is algebraically identical to adding L2 (weight decay) regularisation to MLE. This is a beautiful bridge between two seemingly different ideas: Bayesian prior beliefs and regularisation. A strong prior (small variance $\sigma_0^2$, i.e. strong belief that $\theta \approx 0$) corresponds to large $\lambda$ вҖ” aggressive regularisation that shrinks parameters to zero. With infinite data, the prior is overwhelmed by the likelihood and MAP converges to MLE. With little data, the prior dominates and prevents overfitting. This explains why regularisation works: it encodes the prior belief that model parameters should be small, which is usually correct.

ch03.title: EM Algorithm and Gaussian Mixture Models
ch03.summary: A single Gaussian cannot capture multi-modal data. GMMs mix K Gaussians with different parameters. The EM algorithm provides an elegant iterative procedure: E-step computes soft cluster assignments, M-step re-estimates parameters using those assignments.
ch03.context: Lecture 5 В· 25.10.2023 вҖ” Unsupervised density modelling

sections.heading: Gaussian Mixture Model
sections.text:
$$p(x) = \sum_{k=1}^K \pi_k\,\mathcal{N}(x;\mu_k,\Sigma_k)$$

where $\pi_k \geq 0$ and $\sum_k \pi_k = 1$ (mixing coefficients).
Each component is a soft cluster вҖ” GMMs are universal density approximators.

sections.heading: Why Direct MLE Fails
sections.text: We cannot directly apply MLE to a GMM because the log-likelihood has the form $\sum_n \ln\sum_k \pi_k \mathcal{N}(x_n;\mu_k,\Sigma_k)$ вҖ” a log of a sum, which has no closed-form solution. The fundamental problem is that we don't know which component generated each data point (these are called 'latent variables'). If we knew the assignments (which component $k$ generated $x_n$), we could do MLE easily on each component separately. EM addresses this by alternating: first estimate soft assignments given current parameters (E-step), then maximise expected likelihood given those soft assignments (M-step).

sections.heading: EM Algorithm for GMMs
sections.text:
E-step вҖ” soft assignment (responsibility of component $k$ for point $x_n$):
$$r_{nk} = \frac{\pi_k\,\mathcal{N}(x_n;\mu_k,\Sigma_k)}{\sum_j \pi_j\,\mathcal{N}(x_n;\mu_j,\Sigma_j)}$$

M-step вҖ” update parameters (let $N_k = \sum_n r_{nk}$):
$$\pi_k = \frac{N_k}{N}, \qquad \mu_k = \frac{1}{N_k}\sum_n r_{nk}\,x_n, \qquad \Sigma_k = \frac{1}{N_k}\sum_n r_{nk}(x_n-\mu_k)(x_n-\mu_k)^\top$$

Log-likelihood increases monotonically at each iteration.

sections.heading: Interpreting EM Intuitively
sections.text: In the E-step, $r_{nk}$ is the posterior probability that point $x_n$ was generated by component $k$ вҖ” it tells us how responsible each Gaussian is for each point. A point right in the centre of component 3 will get $r_{n3} \approx 1$, while a point between two components splits its responsibility between them. In the M-step, we do a soft weighted refit: each component's mean is the weighted average of all points, weighted by their responsibility for that component. $N_k = \sum_n r_{nk}$ is the 'effective number of points' assigned to component $k$. The log-likelihood is guaranteed to increase (or stay the same) at each EM step вҖ” this is proven by Jensen's inequality вҖ” so EM always converges, but possibly to a local maximum. Starting from multiple random initialisations and taking the best solution is standard practice.

left.title: K-means
points: Special case of GMM with isotropic ОЈ and hard assignments

right.title: EM / GMM
points: Full density model (can generate, detect outliers)
K-means initialisation is a common good practice

ch04.title: Discriminant Functions and Decision Surfaces
ch04.summary: Linear discriminant functions assign classes via $y(x) = w^\top x + w_0$. The decision boundary $y(x)=0$ is a hyperplane perpendicular to $w$. This simple model is the foundation for SVMs, logistic regression, and linear layers in neural networks.
ch04.context: Lecture 6 В· 08.11.2023 вҖ” From Bayes to direct classifiers

sections.heading: Linear Discriminant Function
sections.text:
$$y(x) = w^\top x + w_0$$

Decision boundary: $y(x) = 0$ вҖ” a hyperplane with:
- Normal vector $w$ (perpendicular to boundary)
- Signed distance from origin: $-w_0/\|w\|$
- Distance from $x$ to boundary: $y(x)/\|w\|$

2 classes: assign $C_1$ if $y(x) > 0$, else $C_2$
$K$ classes: assign $C_k$ if $y_k(x) > y_j(x)\;\forall j\neq k$

sections.heading: Geometry of a Linear Classifier
sections.text: The weight vector $w$ plays two roles simultaneously: it is the normal to the decision hyperplane, and it points in the direction of increasing confidence for class $C_1$. The signed distance from any point $x$ to the boundary is exactly $y(x)/\|w\|$ вҖ” so the value of the discriminant function directly measures how confidently the model classifies $x$. The bias $w_0$ shifts the hyperplane away from the origin; without it, the boundary is forced to pass through the origin, which is unnecessarily restrictive. This geometric picture is crucial for understanding SVMs: they explicitly maximise the distance from the boundary to the nearest data points.

left.title: One-vs-All / One-vs-One
points: Ambiguous regions exist in input space
Each classifier sees different class balance
Simple to implement with binary solvers

right.title: K-class Discriminant
points: Required by softmax / neural net output

sections.heading: Why K-class Discriminant is Preferable
sections.text: In One-vs-All (OvA), classifier $k$ learns to separate class $k$ from all others. But in regions of input space equidistant from all classes, multiple classifiers may fire simultaneously вҖ” or none of them fire вҖ” creating ambiguous zones with no valid prediction. One-vs-One (OvO) trains $K(K-1)/2$ binary classifiers and uses voting, but has similar ambiguity issues plus much higher computational cost for large $K$. A $K$-class discriminant avoids both problems: we assign class $k$ where $y_k(x)$ is largest, giving a single, unambiguous prediction everywhere. The resulting decision regions are guaranteed to be singly-connected convex polytopes. This is why softmax in neural networks works correctly for multi-class problems.

ch04.title: Least-Squares Classification
ch04.summary: Least-squares finds the weight matrix $W$ by minimising the sum of squared differences between model outputs and target labels. It has a closed-form solution via the pseudo-inverse, but is surprisingly poor for classification вҖ” it penalises 'too-correct' predictions.
ch04.context: Lecture 6 В· 08.11.2023 вҖ” A closed-form classifier

sections.heading: Least-Squares Solution
sections.text:
Design matrix $\tilde{X}$ ($N\times(D+1)$, with bias column), target matrix $T$ ($N\times K$, one-hot rows).

Minimise:
$$E_D(W) = \tfrac{1}{2}\,\mathrm{Tr}\bigl\{(\tilde{X}W - T)^\top(\tilde{X}W - T)\bigr\}$$

Setting gradient to zero:
$$W^{\text{LS}} = (\tilde{X}^\top\tilde{X})^{-1}\tilde{X}^\top T = \tilde{X}^\dagger\, T$$

where $\tilde{X}^\dagger$ is the Moore-Penrose pseudo-inverse.

sections.heading: The Pseudo-Inverse and When It Breaks
sections.text: The Moore-Penrose pseudo-inverse $\tilde{X}^\dagger = (\tilde{X}^\top\tilde{X})^{-1}\tilde{X}^\top$ gives the least-squares solution in one shot вҖ” no iterative optimisation needed. This is appealing. However, $(\tilde{X}^\top\tilde{X})$ must be invertible, which requires $N > D+1$ (more data points than parameters). When $D$ is large (e.g. image features), $\tilde{X}^\top\tilde{X}$ is $D\times D$ and inverting it costs $O(D^3)$ вҖ” infeasible for $D > 10^4$. In practice, least-squares classification is rarely used for high-dimensional data; iterative gradient-based methods are preferred.

sections.heading: Why Least-Squares Fails for Classification
sections.text: The squared loss penalises predictions that are too far from the target in either direction. Consider a binary problem with target $t=1$ for class $C_1$. If a point is very obviously in $C_1$ and gets prediction $y=5$, the squared loss is $(5-1)^2=16$ вҖ” heavily penalised despite being 'very correct'. This pulls the decision boundary toward that point, degrading its quality. Logistic regression avoids this: the cross-entropy loss only penalises incorrect predictions and saturates at 0 for confident correct predictions. The second problem is that outliers on the correct side of the boundary distort the boundary badly. This is not hypothetical вҖ” with even a single cluster of outliers far on the correct side, LS can flip the boundary entirely.

ch04.title: Logistic Regression
ch04.summary: Logistic regression models the posterior $p(C_k|x)$ directly as a linear function passed through a sigmoid (or softmax). It is a discriminative model with excellent properties: probabilistic outputs, convex optimisation, and no 'too-correct' problem.
ch04.context: Lectures 6вҖ“7 В· 08вҖ“13.11.2023 вҖ” The discriminative model

sections.heading: Logistic Regression (2 classes)
sections.text:
$$p(C_1\mid x) = \sigma(w^\top x + w_0) = \frac{1}{1+e^{-(w^\top x + w_0)}}$$

Cross-entropy loss (negative log-likelihood):
$$E(w) = -\sum_n\bigl[t_n\ln y_n + (1-t_n)\ln(1-y_n)\bigr], \quad y_n = \sigma(w^\top x_n)$$

No closed form вҖ” optimise with gradient descent or IRLS.

sections.heading: The Sigmoid and Its Role
sections.text: The sigmoid function $\sigma(a) = 1/(1+e^{-a})$ maps any real number to $(0,1)$, making it suitable for outputting a probability. When $a=0$, $\sigma(0)=0.5$ вҖ” maximum uncertainty, at the decision boundary. When $a \to +\infty$, $\sigma \to 1$ (confidently $C_1$). When $a \to -\infty$, $\sigma \to 0$ (confidently $C_2$). The derivative $\sigma'(a) = \sigma(a)(1-\sigma(a))$ is maximised at $a=0$ (slope 0.25) and goes to zero at saturation. This saturation means that for points far from the boundary, the gradient of the loss with respect to $w$ is tiny вҖ” the network 'knows' these points are classified correctly and doesn't waste gradient on them. This is exactly what we want for classification.

sections.heading: Softmax for K Classes
sections.text:
$$p(C_k\mid x) = \mathrm{softmax}(y_k) = \frac{\exp(w_k^\top x)}{\sum_j \exp(w_j^\top x)}$$

Outputs sum to 1 and are all positive вҖ” a valid probability distribution.
Used in multi-class classification and as NN output layer.

sections.heading: Why cross-entropy and not squared loss?
sections.text: Cross-entropy has three critical advantages for classification: (1) It gives zero loss for a perfectly correct prediction with probability 1 вҖ” unlike squared loss which still penalises. (2) Its gradient $\partial E/\partial w = \sum_n(y_n - t_n)x_n$ is proportional to the prediction error $(y_n - t_n)$ вҖ” beautifully clean and large where the model is wrong. (3) The combined objective (cross-entropy with sigmoid output) is convex in $w$ вҖ” guaranteed to have a single global minimum reachable by gradient descent. Squared loss applied to sigmoid output is non-convex and the gradient vanishes where $y_n \approx 0$ or $y_n \approx 1$ even when the prediction is wrong. Cross-entropy is the correct loss for probabilistic classifiers.

sections.heading: IRLS: The Iterative Reweighted Least Squares Connection
sections.text: Although logistic regression has no closed-form solution, it can be solved by IRLS: at each step, form a weighted least-squares problem where the weights are $y_n(1-y_n)$ (the variance of a Bernoulli). This is Newton's method applied to the cross-entropy loss вҖ” it converges in far fewer iterations than gradient descent. In practice, for small datasets IRLS is preferred; for large datasets, mini-batch SGD or Adam is used since Newton's method requires inverting the Hessian.

ch04.title: From Bayes to Logistic: The Bridge
ch04.summary: If class-conditionals are Gaussians with shared covariance (LDA assumptions), applying Bayes theorem gives exactly logistic regression. This unifies generative and discriminative approaches and reveals when each is preferable.
ch04.context: Lectures 6вҖ“7 В· 08вҖ“13.11.2023 вҖ” Unifying generative and discriminative

sections.heading: Derivation: Gaussian posteriors вҶ’ Logistic
sections.text:
Given $p(x\mid C_k) = \mathcal{N}(x;\mu_k,\Sigma)$ (shared covariance):

$$p(C_1\mid x) = \sigma(a), \quad a = \ln\frac{p(C_1\mid x)}{p(C_2\mid x)} = w^\top x + w_0$$

$$w = \Sigma^{-1}(\mu_1-\mu_2), \qquad w_0 = -\tfrac{1}{2}(\mu_1+\mu_2)^\top\Sigma^{-1}(\mu_1-\mu_2) + \ln\frac{p(C_1)}{p(C_2)}$$

Result: Gaussian class-conditionals with shared $\Sigma$ вҶ’ posterior is exactly logistic regression!

sections.heading: Why This Matters
sections.text: This derivation shows that logistic regression is not just some arbitrary model вҖ” it is the exact Bayes-optimal classifier under Gaussian class-conditional assumptions. This means LDA and logistic regression make identical predictions when the Gaussian assumptions hold. They differ only in how they estimate $w$: LDA fits each class density separately (MLE on Gaussians) and derives $w$; logistic regression directly maximises $p(t|X,w)$, which is more efficient because it doesn't waste effort modelling the marginal $p(x)$. The insight also extends: for Gaussians with separate covariances (QDA), the posterior becomes a quadratic function of $x$ вҖ” a quadratic discriminant. For exponential family class-conditionals (Poisson, Bernoulli, etc.), the posterior becomes a generalised linear model.

right.title: Logistic Regression (Discriminative)
points: Cannot generate samples or use unlabeled data

sections.heading: The Sample Efficiency Trade-off
sections.text: With small training data and correct model assumptions (data really is Gaussian with shared covariance), LDA uses the training data more efficiently вҖ” it has fewer effective parameters to estimate. With abundant data, the discriminative model wins: it makes no assumptions about $p(x)$ and directly estimates what's needed for classification. This is the fundamental bias-variance trade-off applied to model selection: strong correct assumptions help when data is scarce, but become irrelevant (and potentially harmful) when data is abundant. In practice, with modern large datasets, discriminative models (logistic regression, neural networks) dominate.

ch04.title: 0'dan Anlat: Linear Discriminant вҶ’ GLM вҶ’ Logistic Regression
ch04.summary: SДұnДұflandДұrma iГ§in ГјГ§ yaklaЕҹДұmДұn kronolojik geliЕҹimi. Linear discriminant neden yetersiz, GLM nasДұl geliЕҹtiriyor, logistic regression neden son adДұm. Her biri bir Г¶ncekinin tam eksikliДҹini Г§Г¶zГјyor.
ch04.context: Lectures 6вҖ“7 В· 08вҖ“13.11.2023 вҖ” BaЕҹtan sona, 0 bilgiyle

sections.heading: SДұnДұflandДұrma Problemi Nedir?
sections.text:
Elimde bir veri noktasДұ var вҖ” mesela bir email. Bu spam mi, deДҹil mi? Ya da bir hastanДұn kan deДҹerleri var, kanser mi, saДҹlДұklДұ mДұ?

Makine Г¶Дҹrenmesinin bu tГјr problemlerde amacДұ Еҹu: elimdeki Г¶zelliklerden (feature'lardan) yola Г§Дұkarak "hangi sДұnДұfa ait?" sorusunu cevaplamak. Bunu yapmanДұn en basit yolu ise iki sДұnДұf arasДұna bir sДұnДұr Г§izmek.

sections.heading: 1. AЕҹama вҖ” En Basit YaklaЕҹДұm: Linear Discriminant
sections.text:
DГјЕҹГјn ki verin 2 boyutlu вҖ” iki feature var. BunlarДұ bir dГјzleme Г§izince belki de iki sДұnДұf gГјzel ayrДұЕҹДұyor. O zaman aralarДұna bir doДҹru Г§izebilirsin. YГјksek boyutta bu doДҹru bir hyperplane oluyor ama fikir aynДұ.

Bu sДұnДұrДұ matematiksel olarak ЕҹГ¶yle yazДұyoruz вҖ” LaTeX olmadan sГ¶ylersem: her feature'Дұ bir aДҹДұrlДұkla Г§arp, topla, bir de sabit ekle. SonuГ§ sДұfДұrdan bГјyГјkse sДұnДұf 1, kГјГ§Гјkse sДұnДұf 2.

Bu "linear discriminant" dediДҹimiz Еҹey. ГҮok gГјzel bir tarafДұ var: kapalДұ form Г§Г¶zГјmГј var, yani bir matris iЕҹlemiyle direkt cevabДұ bulabiliyorsun. Hesaplama aГ§ДұsДұndan Г§ok hДұzlДұ.

Ama iki bГјyГјk sorunu var:

Birinci sorun: ГҮДұktДұ sДұnДұrsДұz. Bir veri noktasДұ iГ§in "3.7" gibi bir sayДұ Г§Дұkabiliyor. Bu ne demek? SДұnДұf 1 olasДұlДұДҹДұ 3.7 mi? AnlamsДұz. OlasДұlДұk 0 ile 1 arasДұnda olmalДұ.

Д°kinci sorun: Outlier'lara aЕҹДұrДұ hassas. EДҹer bir veri noktasДұ Г§ok uzakta ama doДҹru sДұnДұflandДұrДұlmДұЕҹsa, least-squares onu da minimize etmeye Г§alДұЕҹДұyor ve tГјm sДұnДұrДұ kaydДұrДұyor. Lecture'daki slide'da bunu gГ¶sterdiler вҖ” 3 sДұnДұflДұ bir problemde yeЕҹil sДұnДұfДұn bГјyГјk Г§oДҹunluДҹu yanlДұЕҹ sДұnДұflandДұrДұlДұyordu.

Peki ne yapacaДҹДұz?

sections.heading: 2. AЕҹama вҖ” Generalized Linear Model: ГҮДұktДұyДұ SДұkДұЕҹtДұr
sections.text:
Sorun Г§ДұktДұnДұn sДұnДұrsДұz olmasДұ. ГҮГ¶zГјm: Г§ДұktДұyДұ bir fonksiyondan geГ§ir ve 0 ile 1 arasДұna sДұkДұЕҹtДұr.

Bu fonksiyona "aktivasyon fonksiyonu" diyorlar. Lecture'da buna "g()" yazДұyorlar. Yani model artДұk ЕҹГ¶yle: Г¶nce linear kombinasyonu hesapla, sonra g()'den geГ§ir.

Hangi g() seГ§elim? Д°Еҹte burada lecture'da gГјzel bir Еҹey yapДұyorlar. EДҹer iki sДұnДұfДұn her biri Gaussian daДҹДұlДұmlДұysa ve Bayes teoremini uygularsan, posterior olasДұlДұk zaten otomatik olarak sigmoid Еҹeklinde Г§ДұkДұyor diyorlar. Yani sigmoid seГ§imi keyfi deДҹil вҖ” matematiksel olarak tГјretilebiliyor.

Sigmoid neye benziyor? S Еҹeklinde bir eДҹri. ГҮok bГјyГјk pozitif sayДұlarДұ 1'e yakДұn bir deДҹere, Г§ok bГјyГјk negatif sayДұlarДұ 0'a yakДұn bir deДҹere gГ¶tГјrГјyor. Ortada ise dГјzgГјn bir geГ§iЕҹ var. Yani artДұk Г§ДұktДұmДұz gerГ§ekten 0 ile 1 arasДұnda ve olasДұlДұk gibi yorumlanabiliyor.

Bu aЕҹamaya "Generalized Linear Model" diyorlar Г§ГјnkГј sДұnДұrlar hГўlГў linear вҖ” g() monoton olduДҹu iГ§in g(wTx) = sabit demek wTx = baЕҹka bir sabit demek. Karar sДұnДұrДұnДұn Еҹekli deДҹiЕҹmiyor, sadece Г§ДұktДұ yorumu deДҹiЕҹiyor.

Peki sorun Г§Г¶zГјldГј mГј? HayДұr, yeni bir sorun Г§ДұktДұ.

Bu modeli hГўlГў least-squares ile eДҹitiyorlar вҖ” yani tahminim ile gerГ§ek etiket arasДұndaki kare farkДұ minimize et diyorlar. Ama sigmoid + least-squares kГ¶tГј bir kombinasyon. Neden?

Sigmoid'in uГ§ deДҹerlerinde eДҹimi neredeyse sДұfДұr. Yani model Г§ok emin olduДҹunda вҖ” ister doДҹru ister yanlДұЕҹ вҖ” gradient kГјГ§ГјlГјyor ve Г¶Дҹrenme duraksДұyor. En kГ¶tГјsГј Еҹu: model tamamen yanlДұЕҹ tahmin ettiДҹinde bile Г§ok eminse gradient sДұfДұra yakДұn. GГјncelleme olmuyor. Г–Дҹrenme donuyor.

Гңstelik least-squares + sigmoid kombinasyonunun loss landscape'i non-convex вҖ” yani birden fazla local minimum var. Gradient descent nereye dГјЕҹerse oraya takДұlДұp kalabiliyor.

sections.heading: 3. AЕҹama вҖ” Logistic Regression: DoДҹru KaybДұ Kullan
sections.text:
Model deДҹiЕҹmiyor. Yine sigmoid var, yine aynДұ yapДұ. DeДҹiЕҹen tek Еҹey: kayДұp fonksiyonu.

Least-squares yerine cross-entropy kullanДұyoruz. Cross-entropy'nin fikri farklДұ: tahminim doДҹru sДұnДұf iГ§in ne kadar olasДұlДұk verdi sorusunu soruyor. EДҹer gerГ§ek sДұnДұf 1 ama sen 0.01 demiЕҹsen вҖ” Г§ok bГјyГјk kayДұp. 0.99 demiЕҹsen вҖ” neredeyse sДұfДұr kayДұp.

Ve burada sihirli bir Еҹey oluyor: cross-entropy + sigmoid kombinasyonunu gradient aГ§ДұsДұndan hesapladДұДҹДұnda, sigmoid'in tГјrevi algebraically iptal oluyor. Gradient sadece hata Г§arpДұ input kalДұyor. Bu demek ki:

Model ne kadar emin olursa olsun, yanlДұЕҹsa gradient bГјyГјk kalДұyor. Г–Дҹrenme duraksatmДұyor. Loss landscape convex вҖ” tek bir global minimum var, gradient descent oraya ulaЕҹabiliyor.

Logistic regression bu yГјzden GLM'in doДҹru eДҹitilmiЕҹ versiyonu.

sections.heading: ГңГ§ГјnГј Yan Yana Koy
items: Linear Discriminant: Basit, hДұzlДұ, kapalДұ form Г§Г¶zГјm. Ama Г§ДұktДұ sДұnДұrsДұz ve outlier'a hassas.
GLM + sigmoid + least-squares: ГҮДұktДұ artДұk olasДұlДұk gibi. Ama eДҹitim hГўlГў kГ¶tГј вҖ” gradient kayboluyor, non-convex.
Logistic Regression вҖ” GLM + sigmoid + cross-entropy: Her Еҹey dГјzgГјn. Convex, temiz gradient, doДҹru olasДұlДұk yorumu. Bu derste Г¶Дҹretilen doДҹru yol.

sections.heading: Neden Bu SДұrayla Д°Еҹleniyor?
sections.text: Lecture'da bu ГјГ§Гј sДұrayla iЕҹleniyor Г§ГјnkГј her biri bir Г¶ncekinin tam olarak hangi sorununu Г§Г¶zdГјДҹГјnГј gГ¶steriyor. Motivation chain'in tam da anlattДұДҹДұ bu.

ch05.title: Error Functions and Motivation for SVMs
ch05.summary: Among all hyperplanes that correctly classify training data, which generalises best? SVMs answer this by choosing the maximum-margin hyperplane. Comparing error functions reveals that hinge loss is the right shape for maximum-margin classification.
ch05.context: Lectures 9вҖ“10 В· 22вҖ“29.11.2023 вҖ” Why maximum margin?

sections.heading: Comparison of Error Functions
rows: Piecewise linear, ignores correct predictions, sparse

sections.heading: The Hinge Loss Shape
sections.text: Hinge loss $\max(0, 1-ty)$ is zero whenever $ty \geq 1$ вҖ” when the prediction is correct and far enough from the boundary. It only increases linearly when $ty < 1$, i.e., when the sample is inside or on the wrong side of the margin. This has a key consequence: most training points contribute zero gradient вҖ” they are 'free'. Only support vectors (those with $ty < 1$) drive the parameter update. This sparsity is both computationally attractive (fewer active constraints) and statistically attractive (the model ignores easy, clearly-correct examples and focuses on the hard ones near the boundary).

sections.heading: The Margin Intuition
sections.text: For a linearly separable dataset, infinitely many hyperplanes achieve zero training error. Statistical learning theory (VC theory) shows that the generalisation error bound depends on the margin: $\text{test error} \leq \text{train error} + O(1/\text{margin})$. The maximum-margin hyperplane вҖ” the one with the largest gap between classes вҖ” has the lowest guaranteed generalisation error. Intuitively: a hyperplane that barely avoids the training points could shift slightly and misclassify new data. A wide-margin hyperplane has more room вҖ” it is more robust to small perturbations in new test points. This makes maximum margin not just a heuristic but a theoretically motivated choice.

ch05.title: Hard-Margin SVM: Formulation
ch05.summary: The hard-margin SVM finds the maximum-margin hyperplane via a constrained quadratic programme. The solution depends only on a sparse subset of training points вҖ” the support vectors вҖ” which lie exactly on the margin boundary.
ch05.context: Lecture 9 В· 22.11.2023 вҖ” The primal SVM problem

sections.heading: Primal Formulation
sections.text:
Canonical form: $t_n(w^\top x_n + b) \geq 1 \;\forall n$ (rescale $w$ so closest points satisfy equality)

Margin width $= 2/\|w\|$, so maximise margin $=$ minimise $\|w\|^2$:

$$\min_{w,b}\;\tfrac{1}{2}\|w\|^2 \qquad \text{s.t.}\quad t_n(w^\top x_n + b) \geq 1\;\forall n$$

This is a convex QP with a unique global minimum.

sections.heading: Why Margin = 2/вҖ–wвҖ–
sections.text: After canonical rescaling, the two support vector hyperplanes are at $w^\top x + b = +1$ and $w^\top x + b = -1$. The distance between two parallel hyperplanes $w^\top x = c_1$ and $w^\top x = c_2$ is $|c_1 - c_2|/\|w\| = 2/\|w\|$. So maximising the margin means minimising $\|w\|$, or equivalently $\|w\|^2$ (same minimiser). The constraint $t_n(w^\top x_n + b) \geq 1$ says every point must be at least on its side of the margin вҖ” nothing inside the 'street'. This gives a clean quadratic programming problem: convex objective, linear constraints, unique global solution.

sections.heading: Lagrangian Dual
sections.text:
Lagrangian:
$$L_p = \tfrac{1}{2}\|w\|^2 - \sum_n a_n\{t_n y(x_n)-1\}$$

KKT stationarity: $w = \sum_n a_n t_n x_n$, $\;\sum_n a_n t_n = 0$

Dual (substitute back):
$$\max_a\;\sum_n a_n - \tfrac{1}{2}\sum_n\sum_m a_n a_m t_n t_m\, x_n^\top x_m$$
$$\text{s.t.}\quad a_n \geq 0,\quad \sum_n a_n t_n = 0$$

sections.heading: The Dual and the Kernel Trick
sections.text: The dual formulation is crucial for two reasons. First, the dual objective depends on training points only through inner products $x_n^\top x_m$ вҖ” this is what enables the kernel trick (replacing dot products with kernel evaluations). Second, the dual has $N$ variables (one Lagrange multiplier $a_n$ per training point), while the primal has $D$ variables. When $D \gg N$ (more features than examples), the dual is much more efficient to solve. At test time, the prediction is $y(x) = \sum_n a_n t_n x_n^\top x + b$ вҖ” again only inner products with training data appear.

sections.heading: Support Vectors
sections.text: KKT complementarity conditions state $a_n(t_n y(x_n) - 1) = 0$ for all $n$. This means either $a_n = 0$ (the point is correctly classified with margin $> 1$, plays no role in defining $w$) or $t_n y(x_n) = 1$ (the point lies exactly on the margin boundary вҖ” a support vector). The decision boundary $w = \sum_n a_n t_n x_n$ is a weighted sum of support vectors only. Typically only 5вҖ“30% of training points are support vectors, giving a sparse, interpretable model. Remove any non-support-vector and the solution doesn't change; support vectors define the boundary.

ch05.title: Soft-Margin SVM
ch05.summary: Real data is rarely perfectly linearly separable. Slack variables $\xi_n$ allow constraint violations, controlled by hyperparameter $C$. Large $C$ вҶ’ narrow margin, few violations (may overfit). Small $C$ вҶ’ wide margin, many violations (may underfit).
ch05.context: Lecture 10 В· 29.11.2023 вҖ” Handling non-separable data

sections.heading: Soft-Margin Formulation
sections.text:
Introduce slack variables $\xi_n \geq 0$:
$$\min_{w,b,\xi}\;\tfrac{1}{2}\|w\|^2 + C\sum_n\xi_n$$
$$\text{s.t.}\quad t_n(w^\top x_n + b) \geq 1-\xi_n,\quad \xi_n \geq 0$$

Dual changes: $0 \leq a_n \leq C$ (C acts as upper bound on dual variables)

sections.heading: Interpreting Slack Variables
sections.text: The slack variable $\xi_n$ measures how far point $n$ violates the margin constraint: $\xi_n = 0$ means the point is correctly classified outside the margin; $\xi_n \in (0,1)$ means correctly classified but inside the margin; $\xi_n = 1$ means exactly on the decision boundary; $\xi_n > 1$ means misclassified. The total $\sum_n \xi_n$ is an upper bound on the number of training errors. By penalising it with weight $C$, we trade off margin width against training accuracy.

sections.heading: Role of C
sections.text: Large $C$: the slack penalty dominates, so the optimiser strongly avoids any constraint violations. This forces a narrow margin that correctly classifies almost everything (similar to hard-margin SVM). Risk: overfitting to noise or outliers. Small $C$: violations are cheap, so the optimiser prioritises a wide margin even at the cost of misclassifying some training points. This gives more regularisation. The trade-off: $C$ plays the opposite role to $\lambda$ in ridge regression вҖ” large $C$ is low regularisation, small $C$ is high regularisation. Optimal $C$ is found by cross-validation.

ch05.title: Kernel Trick
ch05.summary: The SVM dual depends on training points only through inner products $x_n^\top x_m$. Replacing this with $k(x_n,x_m) = \phi(x_n)^\top\phi(x_m)$ implicitly maps data to a high-dimensional feature space without ever computing the explicit mapping.

sections.heading: The Kernel Trick Idea
sections.text: Suppose we want to classify data that is not linearly separable in the original $D$-dimensional input space. We could map inputs to a higher-dimensional feature space $\phi(x) \in \mathbb{R}^M$ where a linear separator exists. For example, the polynomial kernel $k(x,y) = (x^\top y + 1)^2$ in 2D corresponds to the feature map $\phi(x) = (1, x_1, x_2, x_1^2, x_1x_2, x_2^2)$ вҖ” 6 dimensions. The key observation: the SVM dual only needs $\phi(x_n)^\top\phi(x_m)$ вҖ” never $\phi(x)$ itself. We can compute this inner product directly via $k(x_n, x_m)$ without explicitly constructing $\phi$. For the RBF kernel, $\phi$ is infinite-dimensional, yet we compute $k(x,y)$ with a single scalar operation.

sections.heading: Common Kernels
sections.text:
Polynomial:
$$k(x,y) = (x^\top y + 1)^p$$

RBF / Gaussian:
$$k(x,y) = \exp\!\left(-\frac{\|x-y\|^2}{2\sigma^2}\right)$$
$\sigma$ controls width: small $\sigma$ вҶ’ narrow, high-capacity; large $\sigma$ вҶ’ smooth.

Validity (Mercer's condition): the Gram matrix $K_{nm} = k(x_n,x_m)$ must be positive semi-definite.

sections.heading: Mercer's Condition and Valid Kernels
sections.text: Not every function $k(x,y)$ is a valid kernel. Mercer's theorem states that $k$ is a valid kernel (corresponds to an inner product in some feature space) if and only if the $N\times N$ Gram matrix $K_{nm} = k(x_n,x_m)$ is positive semi-definite for any set of inputs $\{x_1,\ldots,x_N\}$. Valid kernels can be combined: sums and products of valid kernels are valid, scaling by a positive constant is valid, and exponentiation is valid. Invalid kernels (e.g. negative-definite matrices) can lead to non-convex SVM problems with multiple local minima.

left.title: SVM
points: Scales poorly with N (O(NВІ) kernel matrix)

right.title: Logistic Regression
points: Scales well with N via mini-batch SGD

ch06.title: Ensemble Methods and Bagging
ch06.summary: Ensemble methods combine multiple weak models to produce a stronger one. Bagging (Bootstrap Aggregating) reduces variance by training models on random subsamples and averaging вҖ” it works best when base models are complex (low bias, high variance) like decision trees.
ch06.context: Lectures 11вҖ“12 В· 29.11вҖ“04.12.2023 вҖ” From single models to committees

sections.heading: Bagging: Variance Reduction
sections.text:
Train $M$ models $h_1,\ldots,h_M$ on $M$ bootstrap datasets (each $\approx 63\%$ of $N$ points, drawn with replacement).

Ensemble prediction:
$$H(x) = \frac{1}{M}\sum_{m=1}^M h_m(x) \quad \text{(regression)}$$

If errors are uncorrelated:
$$E_{\text{ensemble}} = \frac{E_{\text{individual}}}{M} \qquad \text{($M$-fold variance reduction)}$$

sections.heading: Why Bootstrap Sampling?
sections.text: If we had access to unlimited training data, we'd train $M$ models on $M$ independent datasets. We don't вҖ” so we simulate independent datasets by sampling with replacement from the single training set. Each bootstrap dataset has $N$ points drawn with replacement, so about $63\%$ of unique original points appear (some are duplicated, some are left out). The 37% that don't appear in a bootstrap sample are the 'out-of-bag' examples вҖ” they can be used to estimate test error without a separate validation set. The key insight is that models trained on different bootstrap subsets make somewhat uncorrelated errors, so averaging cancels out much of the noise.

sections.heading: The Math Behind Variance Reduction
sections.text: If each model has variance $\sigma^2$ and the models are perfectly uncorrelated, the average of $M$ models has variance $\sigma^2/M$. In practice, models trained on overlapping bootstrap samples are correlated (correlation $\rho > 0$), so the ensemble variance is $\rho\sigma^2 + (1-\rho)\sigma^2/M$. As $M\to\infty$, this converges to $\rho\sigma^2$, not 0 вҖ” the correlation between models is the fundamental bottleneck. Random Forests reduce $\rho$ by also randomising which features are considered at each split, further reducing the ensemble variance.

left.title: Helps (high variance models)
points: Deep decision trees (low bias, high variance)
Unstable learners sensitive to small data changes
Random Forests extend bagging with feature randomness

right.title: Doesn't help (low variance models)
points: Linear models (already stable вҖ” same solution regardless of bootstrap)
Models that don't change much with bootstrap

ch06.title: AdaBoost Algorithm
ch06.summary: AdaBoost (Freund & Schapire, 1996) builds a strong classifier by sequentially training weak learners on reweighted data, focusing each new learner on the mistakes of previous ones. The final model is a weighted majority vote.
ch06.context: Lecture 11 В· 29.11.2023 вҖ” Sequential ensemble learning

sections.heading: AdaBoost Algorithm
sections.text:
Given: $\{(x_n, t_n)\}$, $t_n \in \{-1, +1\}$

1. Initialise: $w_n^{(1)} = 1/N\;\forall n$

2. For $m = 1,\ldots,M$:
   a. Train weak learner $h_m$ on weighted data
   b. Weighted error: $\varepsilon_m = \sum_n w_n^{(m)}\cdot\mathbf{1}[h_m(x_n) \neq t_n]$
   c. Learner weight: $\displaystyle\alpha_m = \frac{1}{2}\ln\frac{1-\varepsilon_m}{\varepsilon_m}$
   d. Update weights: $w_n^{(m+1)} \propto w_n^{(m)}\cdot\exp\!\bigl(\alpha_m\cdot\mathbf{1}[h_m(x_n)\neq t_n]\bigr)$

3. Final: $H(x) = \mathrm{sign}\!\left(\sum_m \alpha_m h_m(x)\right)$

sections.heading: What Counts as a Weak Learner?
sections.text: A weak learner need only be slightly better than random вҖ” for binary classification, it must achieve weighted error $\varepsilon_m < 0.5$. 'Decision stumps' (single-feature threshold classifiers: 'is $x_j > \theta$?') are the canonical example. They're fast to train, extremely simple, and always slightly better than chance on any weighted distribution. The power of AdaBoost is that even this modest requirement is sufficient: the VC theorem for boosting guarantees that the ensemble training error goes to zero exponentially fast, and вҖ” more remarkably вҖ” test error also decreases even after training error is 0.

sections.heading: Intuition Behind Weight Updates
sections.text: Misclassified samples get weight multiplied by $e^{\alpha_m} > 1$ (increased). Correctly classified samples get weight divided (decreased). The next learner therefore focuses on hard examples. The learner weight $\alpha_m = \frac{1}{2}\ln\frac{1-\varepsilon_m}{\varepsilon_m}$: if $\varepsilon_m = 0.5$ (no better than random), $\alpha_m = 0$ вҖ” that learner is ignored entirely. If $\varepsilon_m \to 0$ (perfect), $\alpha_m \to \infty$ вҖ” it dominates the ensemble. Better learners get more say, and learners that are worse than random ($\varepsilon_m > 0.5$) get negative weight вҖ” their predictions are flipped. This automatic weighting makes AdaBoost self-calibrating.

ch06.title: AdaBoost as Exponential Loss Minimisation
ch06.summary: AdaBoost can be derived as gradient descent on the exponential loss вҖ” not just a heuristic. This theoretical unification spawned the gradient boosting family and explains AdaBoost's sensitivity to outliers.

sections.heading: Exponential Loss Connection
sections.text:
AdaBoost minimises the exponential loss:
$$E = \sum_n \exp\!\bigl(-t_n f_m(x_n)\bigr), \quad f_m(x) = \sum_{j=1}^{m}\alpha_j h_j(x)$$

The weight update $w_n^{(m+1)} \propto \exp(-t_n f_{m-1}(x_n))$ is exactly the negative gradient of $E$!

At each step we greedily minimise $E$ by adding the best new weak learner $h_m$ and weight $\alpha_m$.

sections.heading: Why Exponential Loss? Sensitivity to Outliers
sections.text: The exponential loss $\exp(-ty)$ grows exponentially for confident wrong predictions (when $ty \ll 0$). Compare this to hinge loss (linear in $ty$ for wrong predictions) or cross-entropy (logarithmic). This means outliers вҖ” points that are inherently wrong in any sensible model вҖ” get exponentially large weights as AdaBoost focuses on them, eventually dominating the learning. AdaBoost is therefore very sensitive to label noise. A single mislabeled training point will receive exponentially growing weight through the iterations. Gradient boosting with a more robust loss (e.g. logistic loss) addresses this, trading some speed for noise robustness.

sections.heading: The Gradient Boosting Generalisation
sections.text: The exponential-loss interpretation opens a door: what if we used a different loss function and did gradient descent in function space? This is gradient boosting вҖ” at each step, fit a new weak learner to the negative gradient of the loss (the 'pseudo-residuals'). With squared loss, this is simply fitting to residuals (hence Residual Boosting). With absolute loss, it's more robust. XGBoost, LightGBM, and CatBoost вҖ” the dominant tabular data methods in competitions today вҖ” are all gradient boosting with decision trees as weak learners, building directly on this AdaBoost theory.

ch07.title: From Perceptron to MLP: History
ch07.summary: Neural networks have a rich history of hype and setbacks. The Rosenblatt perceptron (1957) started AI optimism; Minsky & Papert (1969) showed its limits with XOR; backpropagation (Rumelhart 1986) revived the field; deep learning (2012+) transformed it.
ch07.context: Lecture 12 В· 04.12.2023 вҖ” Historical and conceptual foundations

sections.heading: Key Milestones
rows: First mathematical model of a neuron
Proved XOR impossible вҶ’ AI winter #1
Transformers dominate NLP and then everything

sections.heading: The XOR Problem
sections.text: A single-layer perceptron can only compute linearly separable functions вҖ” it draws one straight line in input space. XOR is not linearly separable: the four points $(0,0)\to 0$, $(0,1)\to 1$, $(1,0)\to 1$, $(1,1)\to 0$ cannot be separated by any single hyperplane. Minsky and Papert proved this formally in 1969 and it effectively ended neural network research for over a decade. The fix is simple: add one hidden layer. The hidden units can learn intermediate representations that ARE linearly separable. For XOR, two hidden ReLU neurons can compute an intermediate feature that makes the problem trivially linear. This demonstrated that depth enables non-linear function composition вҖ” the foundational insight of deep learning.

sections.heading: Why 2012 Was Different
sections.text: AlexNet's ImageNet breakthrough in 2012 was not a new algorithm вҖ” backpropagation had existed since 1986. What changed: (1) Datasets: ImageNet provided 1.2 million labeled images, enough to train deep nets without overfitting. (2) GPUs: parallel computation made training feasible (AlexNet trained on two GTX 580 GPUs for 5-6 days). (3) ReLU activation replaced sigmoid/tanh, eliminating vanishing gradients in practice. (4) Dropout provided regularisation for the large overparameterised network. All four elements needed to come together. Deep learning's success is as much about data and compute as it is about algorithmic innovation.

ch07.title: Multi-Layer Perceptron (MLP)
ch07.summary: An MLP stacks layers of linear transformations followed by non-linear activations. Without non-linearities, a deep net collapses to a single linear transformation. The universal approximation theorem guarantees expressiveness.

sections.heading: Forward Pass
sections.text:
Layer $l$ computation:
$$z^{(l)} = W^{(l)} a^{(l-1)} + b^{(l)} \qquad \text{(pre-activation)}$$
$$a^{(l)} = g\bigl(z^{(l)}\bigr) \qquad \text{(post-activation, elementwise)}$$

Full network: $y = f(x;W,b) = a^{(L)}$

Without non-linearity ($g = \mathrm{id}$):
$$y = W^{(L)}\cdots W^{(1)} x = W_{\text{eff}}\,x \quad \text{(collapses to linear!)}$$

sections.heading: The Universal Approximation Theorem
sections.text: The universal approximation theorem (Cybenko 1989, Hornik 1991) states: a neural network with one hidden layer of sufficient width and a non-linear activation can approximate any continuous function on a compact domain to arbitrary precision. This guarantees that MLPs are expressive enough вҖ” they are universal. However, 'sufficient width' can be exponentially large in the dimension of the input. Deep networks with moderate width can achieve the same expressiveness with far fewer neurons вҖ” depth buys exponential expressiveness at polynomial cost. This is why depth matters: a function requiring $2^D$ neurons with one layer may need only $O(D^2)$ neurons with multiple layers.

sections.heading: Activation Functions
rows: Sparse, no saturation, dying neurons problem
Fixes dying ReLU with small negative slope

sections.heading: Why ReLU dominates
sections.text: ReLU $\max(0,z)$ has gradient exactly 0 or 1 вҖ” no attenuation through depth. Compare sigmoid: $\sigma'(z) = \sigma(z)(1-\sigma(z)) \leq 0.25$, so each layer multiplies the gradient by at most 0.25. After 10 layers, the gradient is at most $0.25^{10} \approx 10^{-6}$ вҖ” vanished. ReLU's gradient of exactly 1 means it doesn't attenuate gradients for active neurons. The 'dying ReLU' problem (a neuron stuck at $z=0$ for all inputs) is addressed by Leaky ReLU, ELU, or careful initialisation. In modern practice, variants like GELU (used in Transformers) and SiLU are preferred over plain ReLU.

ch07.title: Backpropagation
ch07.summary: Backpropagation applies the chain rule layer by layer from output to input. One forward pass computes all activations; one backward pass computes all gradients. Total cost is only twice the forward pass вҖ” making deep network training feasible.
ch07.context: Lectures 12вҖ“13 В· 04вҖ“11.12.2023 вҖ” How networks learn

sections.heading: The Computational Graph View
sections.text: Any differentiable computation can be represented as a directed acyclic graph (DAG): nodes are operations, edges are data flow. The forward pass computes the output by traversing the graph from inputs to outputs, caching intermediate results. The backward pass traverses the graph in reverse, computing the gradient of the loss with respect to each intermediate value using the chain rule. Each node only needs to know its local gradient (how its output changes with its inputs) вҖ” backpropagation chains these local gradients together. This is exactly what makes automatic differentiation (autograd in PyTorch/TensorFlow) work: you define the forward computation, and the gradient is computed automatically.

sections.heading: Delta Rule (Error Signals)
sections.text:
Define error signal: $\delta^{(l)} = \partial\mathcal{L}/\partial z^{(l)}$

Output layer:
$$\delta^{(L)} = \frac{\partial\mathcal{L}}{\partial a^{(L)}} \odot g'(z^{(L)})$$

Hidden layer (backward recursion):
$$\delta^{(l)} = \bigl(W^{(l+1)}\bigr)^\top \delta^{(l+1)} \odot g'(z^{(l)})$$

Gradients for update:
$$\frac{\partial\mathcal{L}}{\partial W^{(l)}} = \delta^{(l)}\bigl(a^{(l-1)}\bigr)^\top, \qquad \frac{\partial\mathcal{L}}{\partial b^{(l)}} = \delta^{(l)}$$

sections.heading: Computational Efficiency
sections.text: Computing gradients naively (perturbing each weight by $\epsilon$ and measuring output change) would require one forward pass per parameter вҖ” for a network with $10^8$ parameters, this is $10^8$ forward passes per gradient step. Backpropagation computes ALL gradients in a single forward + backward pass. The backward pass cost is at most 2вҖ“3Г— the forward pass. This $O(1)$ cost scaling with parameters (versus $O(P)$ for finite differences) is what makes training deep networks feasible. The 'trick' is reusing intermediate results from the forward pass to avoid redundant computation.

sections.heading: Vanishing and Exploding Gradients
sections.text: In deep nets, gradients pass through $L$ layers via $\delta^{(l)} = (W^{(l+1)})^\top \delta^{(l+1)} \odot g'(z^{(l)})$. Each layer multiplies the gradient by $W^\top$ (magnification) and by $g'(z)$ (activation derivative). For sigmoid, $g'(z) \leq 0.25$, and if $\|W\| < 4$, the product shrinks: gradients vanish at early layers. If $\|W\| > 4$, they explode. Solutions: ReLU activation ($g'=0$ or $1$, no attenuation from activation), residual connections (gradient bypass), batch normalisation (keeps activations in good range), and Glorot/He weight initialisation (sets $\|W\|$ appropriately at initialisation).

ch07.title: Loss Functions for Neural Networks
ch07.summary: Output activation and loss function are always paired: sigmoid + binary cross-entropy for binary classification, softmax + categorical cross-entropy for multi-class, linear + MSE for regression. Each pairing gives clean gradient formulas.
ch07.context: Lectures 12вҖ“13 В· 04вҖ“11.12.2023 вҖ” Objectives and outputs

sections.heading: The clean gradient formula
sections.text: Pairing sigmoid with binary cross-entropy gives the output delta $\delta = \hat{y} - y$ вҖ” the prediction error. This is not a coincidence. The sigmoid activation has derivative $\sigma'(z) = \sigma(z)(1-\sigma(z))$. The cross-entropy loss has derivative $\partial E / \partial \hat{y} = -t/\hat{y} + (1-t)/(1-\hat{y})$. Multiplying these (chain rule through the output layer) gives exactly $\hat{y} - t$. The $\sigma'$ in the denominator exactly cancels with the $1/\hat{y}$ and $1/(1-\hat{y})$ in the loss gradient. This cancellation is a consequence of the sigmoid being the canonical link function for the Bernoulli distribution (exponential family). The clean gradient means no vanishing at the output layer even when $\hat{y} \approx 0$ or $\hat{y} \approx 1$ вҖ” unlike squared loss with sigmoid.

sections.heading: When to Use Which Loss
sections.text: MSE (squared error) is natural for regression: it corresponds to assuming Gaussian noise in the output. It heavily penalises large errors. Cross-entropy is natural for classification: it corresponds to the negative log-likelihood of a categorical distribution. It penalises confident wrong predictions logarithmically. If you use MSE for classification, you get poor gradients (activation derivative kills them for confident wrong predictions) and penalise 'too-correct' predictions. If you use cross-entropy for regression, you implicitly assume discrete outputs. The pairing is not arbitrary вҖ” it comes from the exponential family framework and maximum likelihood.

ch07.title: 0'dan Anlat: Activation вҶ’ Gradient вҶ’ Backprop вҶ’ Training Loop
ch07.summary: Activation fonksiyonu neden gerekli, sigmoid neden baЕҹarДұsДұz, ReLU neden standart, gradient nasДұl Г¶Дҹretiyor, backprop nasДұl Г§alДұЕҹДұyor вҖ” sДұfДұr bilgiden tam tabloya.
ch07.context: Lectures 12вҖ“13 В· 04вҖ“11.12.2023 вҖ” SДұfДұrdan tam tablo

sections.heading: Neden Activation Fonksiyonu Var?
sections.text:
Bir nГ¶ron Еҹunu yapДұyor: tГјm girdileri kendi aДҹДұrlДұklarДұyla Г§arpДұp topluyor, bias ekliyor. SonuГ§ herhangi bir sayДұ olabilir вҖ” buna pre-activation diyoruz. Sonra bu sayДұ activation fonksiyonundan geГ§iyor.

Activation olmadan ne olur? Her katman bir linear dГ¶nГјЕҹГјm yapДұyor. Ama iki linear dГ¶nГјЕҹГјmГј arka arkaya yapДұnca yine bir linear dГ¶nГјЕҹГјm Г§ДұkДұyor. 10 katman da koysam hepsi matematiksel olarak tek bir katmana eЕҹdeДҹer вҖ” derinlik anlamsДұz. Activation araya girerek non-linearity katДұyor, katmanlarДұ Гјst Гјste koymanДұn anlamДұ oluyor.

sections.heading: Sigmoid'in Sorunu
sections.text:
Sigmoid her sayДұyДұ 0 ile 1 arasДұna sДұkДұЕҹtДұrДұyor. ГҮДұktДұ katmanДұnda binary classification iГ§in ideal. Ama hidden layer'larda bГјyГјk sorun var.

Backprop'ta gradient geriye akarken her katmanda o katmanДұn local gradient'iyle Г§arpДұlДұyor. Sigmoid'in local gradient'i en fazla 0.25. 10 katmanlДұ bir aДҹda:

1.0 Г— 0.25 Г— 0.25 Г— ... Г— 0.25 = yaklaЕҹДұk 0.000001

Д°lk katmana ulaЕҹan gradient milyonda bir olmuЕҹ. O katmanДұn weight'leri neredeyse hiГ§ gГјncellenemiyor. Д°lk katmanlar Г¶Дҹrenemiyor вҖ” buna vanishing gradient diyorlar.

sections.heading: ReLU Neden Daha Д°yi?
sections.text:
ReLU basit: pre-activation pozitifse olduДҹu gibi bДұrak, negatifse sДұfДұr yap.

Pozitif bГ¶lgede local gradient 1. Backprop'ta:

1.0 Г— 1 Г— 1 Г— ... Г— 1 = 1.0

Gradient ilk katmana orijinal bГјyГјklГјДҹГјnde ulaЕҹДұyor. TГјm katmanlar Г¶Дҹrenebiliyor.

"Ama nГ¶ronlarДұn yarДұsДұ 0 Гјretmiyor mu?" вҖ” bu aslДұnda iyi bir Еҹey. FarklДұ nГ¶ronlar farklДұ pattern'lere uzmanlaЕҹДұyor. "Yatay kenar" Г¶Дҹrenen nГ¶ron araba resmi iГ§in ateЕҹlenmez вҖ” bu normal, o nГ¶ronun iЕҹi bu deДҹil. Her input iГ§in farklДұ nГ¶ronlar ateЕҹleniyor, bu efficient bir representation.

GerГ§ek sorun "dying ReLU" вҖ” bir nГ¶ron hiГ§bir input iГ§in hiГ§ ateЕҹlenmiyorsa kalДұcДұ olarak Г¶lГјyor, hiГ§ gradient almДұyor. Bu Г§ok bГјyГјk learning rate kullandДұДҹДұnda olabilir, ama iyi ayarlanmДұЕҹ eДҹitimde nadir.

sections.heading: Gradient Ne Demek, NasДұl Г–Дҹretiyor?
sections.text:
Gradient bir fonksiyonun herhangi bir noktadaki eДҹimi вҖ” her yГ¶nde zemin nasДұl eДҹimli. Loss'u minimize etmek istiyoruz, gradient loss'un en hДұzlДұ arttДұДҹДұ yГ¶nГј gГ¶steriyor, biz ters yГ¶ne gidiyoruz.

Somut Г¶rnek: bir weight w iГ§in gradient 3 Г§ДұktДұ. Bu demek ki w'yu artДұrДұrsan loss 3 kat artДұyor. O zaman w'yu azalt:

yeni w = eski w - learning_rate Г— gradient
yeni w = w - 0.1 Г— 3 = w - 0.3

Gradient negatif Г§ДұksaydДұ update pozitif olurdu, w artardДұ. Her zaman loss'u dГјЕҹГјren yГ¶ne gidiyorsun.

sections.heading: Training Loop вҖ” BГјyГјk Resim
sections.text:
Weight'ler rastgele baЕҹlatДұlДұyor. Bir adДұm Еҹunlardan oluЕҹuyor:

1. Forward pass вҖ” veri tГјm katmanlardan geГ§iyor. Д°lk adДұmda weight'ler random, tahmin saГ§ma. Kedi resmini gГ¶rdГј, "kГ¶pek %73" dedi. Bu adДұmda Г¶Дҹrenme yok вҖ” sadece tahmin Гјretiliyor ve her katmanДұn ara deДҹerleri hafДұzada saklanДұyor. Bu saklama Г¶nemli, backprop bunlara ihtiyaГ§ duyacak.

2. Loss hesaplanДұyor вҖ” network'Гјn son katmanДұndan bir olasДұlДұk vektГ¶rГј Г§ДұkДұyor. GerГ§ek etiketle karЕҹДұlaЕҹtДұrДұlДұp tek bir sayДұ Гјretiliyor вҖ” mesela 2.3. Loss tek bir scalar.

3. Backprop вҖ” tek bir geriye geГ§iЕҹte tГјm weight'lerin gradientleri hesaplanДұyor. Г–Дҹrenme henГјz yok, sadece hesaplama.

4. Weight update вҖ” tГјm weight'ler aynДұ anda gГјncelleniyor. Г–Дҹrenme burada.

Bitti. Bir adДұm bu. Sonra baЕҹa dГ¶n, aynДұ Еҹeyi tekrar yap.

sections.heading: Output ve Loss NasДұl ГҮДұkДұyor?
sections.text:
Network'Гјn son katmanДұndan bir olasДұlДұk vektГ¶rГј Г§ДұkДұyor. Mesela 3 sДұnДұflДұ bir problemde softmax sonrasДұ:

Kedi:   0.72
KГ¶pek:  0.20
KuЕҹ:    0.08

GerГ§ek etiket kedi. Cross-entropy bu ikisini karЕҹДұlaЕҹtДұrДұp tek bir sayДұ Гјretiyor вҖ” mesela 2.3. Loss tek bir scalar. VektГ¶r deДҹil, matris deДҹil. Sadece model ne kadar yanlДұЕҹ bilgisi.

sections.heading: Tek Bir SayДұdan Milyonlarca Gradient NasДұl ГҮДұkДұyor?
sections.text:
Loss tek sayДұ, ama loss'un output'a gГ¶re tГјrevi bir vektГ¶r:

Loss = 2.3  (tek sayДұ)
dLoss/dOutput = [-0.28, +0.20, +0.08]  (vektГ¶r)

Bu vektГ¶r kedi tahminini artДұrДұrsan loss azalДұr, kГ¶pek ve kuЕҹu azaltДұrsan loss azalДұr diyor. Bilgi burada aГ§ДұlДұyor.

Backprop bu vektГ¶rГј alДұp geriye doДҹru katman katman yГјrГјyor. Her katmanda Еҹu soruyu soruyor: "Bir Г¶nceki katmanДұn Г§ДұktДұsДұ biraz deДҹiЕҹseydi, loss ne kadar deДҹiЕҹirdi?"

Ve her katman sadece iki Еҹeye bakДұyor:

Birincisi, bir sonraki katmandan gelen gradient вҖ” "benden sonraki kДұsДұm loss'u ne kadar etkiliyor?"

Д°kincisi, kendi forward pass'te sakladДұДҹДұ ara deДҹer вҖ” "ben o adДұmda ne ГјretmiЕҹtim?"

Bu ikisini Г§arpДұyor, kendi weight'lerinin gradient'ini buluyor, ve bir Г¶nceki katmana gradient'i iletiyor:

wвӮҒ вҶ’ katman1 вҶ’ hвӮҒ вҶ’ katman2 вҶ’ hвӮӮ вҶ’ katman3 вҶ’ output вҶ’ loss

Backprop:
loss вҶ’ katman3'Гјn gradient'i bulundu вҶ’ hвӮӮ'ye iletildi
hвӮӮ  вҶ’ katman2'nin gradient'i bulundu вҶ’ hвӮҒ'e iletildi
hвӮҒ  вҶ’ katman1'in gradient'i bulundu вҶ’ wвӮҒ gГјncellendi

Sigmoid'de her katmanda 0.25 ile Г§arpДұlДұyor, 10 katman sonra milyonda bir. ReLU'da 1 ile Г§arpДұlДұyor, ilk katmana gГјГ§lГј ulaЕҹДұyor.

Tek sayДұdan bilgi Г§ДұkmДұyor aslДұnda вҖ” bilgi zaten zincirin iГ§inde saklДұydДұ. Backprop o zinciri geriye doДҹru takip ederek her weight'in katkДұsДұnДұ aГ§ДұДҹa Г§ДұkarДұyor.

sections.heading: Г–Дҹrenme Nerede, Ne Zaman Oluyor?
sections.text:
Forward pass  вҶ’  tahmin Гјret + ara deДҹerleri sakla
Loss          вҶ’  ne kadar yanlДұЕҹДұm? (tek bir sayДұ)
Backprop      вҶ’  her weight loss'u ne kadar etkiliyor?
