This cancellation prevents gradient saturation at the output layer and is why these specific pairings are always used together.

Lab baДҹlantДұsДұ вҖ” Lab Session 3 (Neural Network & Backprop): MLP'yi tamamen sДұfДұrdan NumPy ile implement etme вҖ” hiГ§ PyTorch/Keras yok. Linear layer forward ve backward pass manuel yazДұlДұyor, MSE loss, sigmoid aktivasyonu ve tГјrevi, SGD gГјncelleme adДұmlarДұ. Sonra SoftMax + Cross Entropy eklenerek MNIST Гјzerinde eДҹitim. Backprop'un her adДұmДұnДұ elle gГ¶rmek iГ§in en doДҹrudan lab.
overviews.ch08:
Training a neural network is a non-convex optimisation problem over millions of parameters. Unlike the convex objectives of logistic regression and SVMs, there is no guarantee of reaching the global minimum. Yet deep networks achieve remarkable performance. This chapter explains the practical optimisation techniques that make this possible.

Gradient descent updates $w \leftarrow w - \eta\nabla_w E(w)$. The learning rate $\eta$ is the most critical hyperparameter: too large causes divergence; too small causes impractically slow convergence. Batch GD uses all $N$ training points for an exact gradient but is prohibitively expensive for large datasets. SGD uses one random point per update вҖ” noisy, but fast and scalable. Mini-batch SGD (batch size $B \approx 32$вҖ“$512$) strikes the practical balance: fast iterations, GPU-parallelisable computation, and moderate noise. Importantly, the noise in SGD gradients is not just a necessary evil вҖ” it acts as implicit regularisation, steering the optimiser toward flat minima (wide loss basins) that generalise better than the sharp minima found by exact gradient descent.

Plain SGD struggles with ill-conditioned landscapes: it oscillates across steep dimensions while barely moving along flat ones. Momentum accumulates gradient history $v \leftarrow \beta v + (1-\beta)\nabla E$, then updates $w \leftarrow w - \eta v$ ($\beta \approx 0.9$). Momentum dampens oscillations along steep dimensions (alternating gradients cancel) and accelerates along flat ones (consistent gradients accumulate). Adam (Adaptive Moment Estimation) goes further: it maintains a running mean $m_t$ and running variance $v_t$ of gradients, dividing the update by $\sqrt{\hat{v}_t}$. This gives each parameter its own effective learning rate вҖ” large for sparse/small-gradient parameters (word embeddings for rare words), small for large-gradient parameters (to prevent oscillation). After bias correction for the initialisation at zero, Adam converges reliably with default hyperparameters ($\beta_1=0.9$, $\beta_2=0.999$, $\eta=10^{-3}$) across nearly all deep learning architectures. It is the standard optimiser.

Batch Normalisation (Ioffe & Szegedy, 2015) addresses internal covariate shift: as earlier layers update, the distribution of their outputs shifts, forcing later layers to constantly adapt. BN normalises each layer's activations to zero mean and unit variance within each mini-batch, then rescales with learnable parameters $\gamma$ and $\beta$: $y_i = \gamma\hat{x}_i + \beta$ where $\hat{x}_i = (x_i - \mu_{\mathcal{B}})/\sqrt{\sigma^2_{\mathcal{B}}+\varepsilon}$. This stabilises the input distribution to each layer, enabling higher learning rates, better gradient flow (activations stay away from sigmoid saturation), and mild regularisation from batch statistics noise. At test time, running averages of training statistics replace per-batch statistics вҖ” the reason models must be switched between train and eval modes.

Weight initialisation determines whether gradients vanish or explode from the very first step. Glorot initialisation $\text{Var}(W) = 2/(n_{\text{in}}+n_{\text{out}})$ keeps signal variance approximately constant through the network for symmetric activations. He initialisation doubles this for ReLU, which zeros half its inputs. Dropout randomly deactivates neurons with probability $p$ during training, forcing redundancy: no single neuron can be exclusively relied upon. At test time, all neurons are active with weights scaled by $(1-p)$. Interpreted as an ensemble of $2^N$ subnetworks, dropout is one of the most effective regularisers for fully-connected layers. Batch normalisation often reduces the need for dropout in convolutional networks.

Lab baДҹlantДұsДұ вҖ” Lab Session 2 (Loss & Metric): PyTorch ve TorchMetrics ile loss fonksiyonlarДұ ve metrikler. Regresyon iГ§in MSE/MAE, sДұnДұflandДұrma iГ§in CE/BCE, segmentasyon iГ§in Dice coefficient, binary classification iГ§in ROC + AUROC. GГ¶rГјntГј kalitesi iГ§in SSIM. Hangi loss'un hangi durumda kullanДұldДұДҹДұnДұ ve neden seГ§ildiДҹini karЕҹДұlaЕҹtДұrmalДұ gГ¶rme imkГўnДұ.
overviews.ch09:
Applying fully-connected networks to images is both computationally infeasible and statistically counterproductive. A single FC layer for a $224\times224\times3$ image has 150 million parameters вҖ” just for one layer. More fundamentally, FC layers ignore the structure of images: moving a cat one pixel to the right changes all 150k inputs, requiring the network to re-learn 'cat recognition' for every spatial position. CNNs solve this by encoding three domain-specific properties directly into the architecture.

Natural images exhibit locality (nearby pixels are correlated; distant ones are often independent), translation equivariance (a cat looks the same whether top-left or bottom-right), and hierarchical composition (Hubel & Wiesel, 1959: simple cells detect oriented edges; complex cells detect the same edge anywhere; higher areas detect shapes and objects). CNNs implement these through convolutional layers (local filters with weight sharing: the same $3\times3$ filter slides across the entire image), pooling (local translation invariance and spatial downsampling), and layer stacking (hierarchical feature composition). A $3\times3$ filter with $C_{\text{in}}$ input channels has $9C_{\text{in}}$ parameters, regardless of image size. With 64 filters: $9\times64\times C_{\text{in}}$ parameters vs $64\times W\times H\times C_{\text{in}}$ for FC вҖ” a $W\times H/9$ reduction, typically $5000\times$ fewer parameters.

Stacking convolutional layers grows the receptive field without growing the parameter count: $L$ layers of $3\times3$ filters cover a $(2L+1)\times(2L+1)$ input region. Early layers detect edges and textures; middle layers detect shapes and parts; deep layers detect semantic categories. This hierarchy mirrors the brain's visual cortex and is what makes CNNs work: relevant features are progressively abstracted. Pooling (typically $2\times2$ max pooling with stride 2) downsamples the feature map by $4\times$ while providing local translation invariance. Modern architectures increasingly replace pooling with strided convolutions (learnable downsampling).

CNN architectures evolved rapidly by addressing successive bottlenecks. AlexNet (2012): ReLU activations (no vanishing gradients), GPU training, dropout regularisation вҖ” first to beat classical features at scale. VGGNet (2014): systematic $3\times3$ convolutions, same receptive field as larger kernels with fewer parameters and more non-linearity. GoogLeNet (2014): Inception modules with parallel branches of different kernel sizes вҖ” the network learns which scale to use. ResNet (2015): residual connections $\text{output} = F(x) + x$ solved the degradation problem вҖ” deeper networks were getting worse, not due to overfitting but due to optimisation difficulty. Skip connections provide gradient highways and make learning the identity easy: if $F(x)\approx0$, the output is simply $x$. ResNets enabled 50, 100, 152-layer networks and human-level ImageNet accuracy.

Object detection extends classification to localisation: predict both 'what' and 'where'. The R-CNN family progressively optimised this: R-CNN (apply CNN to 2000 proposals, 47s/image) вҶ’ Fast R-CNN (extract features once, RoI pooling per region, 2s/image) вҶ’ Faster R-CNN (learn proposals with RPN, end-to-end, 0.2s/image). YOLO (2016) reformulated detection as a single regression problem: a $S\times S$ grid, each cell predicting $B$ bounding boxes and class probabilities in one forward pass вҖ” 45 FPS real-time detection. The two-stage vs single-stage accuracy-speed trade-off has been progressively closing; DETR (2020) replaced hand-engineered anchors and NMS with Transformers and bipartite matching, unifying detection with the same architecture used for NLP.

Lab baДҹlantДұsДұ вҖ” Lab Session 4 + Session 5: Session 4'te Pascal VOC 2012 Гјzerinde VGG-16 multi-label classification (VGG block sДұfДұrdan) ve BasicResnetBlock ile skip connection implement etme, Top-1/Top-5 accuracy. Session 5'te aynДұ dataset Гјzerinde semantic segmentation вҖ” VGG'nin FC katmanlarДұnДұ Conv'a dГ¶nГјЕҹtГјrerek FCN yapma, transposed convolution ile upsampling, skip connection ile multi-scale feature fusion, mIoU ve Dice score deДҹerlendirme. Bonus: tam U-Net mimarisi.
overviews.ch10:
All models so far treat inputs as real-valued vectors with inherent geometry. Text is different: words are discrete symbolic tokens. One-hot encodings give no notion of similarity вҖ” 'dog' and 'cat' are as distant as 'dog' and 'democracy'. Word embeddings solve this by mapping each word to a continuous vector where geometric proximity reflects semantic similarity. This chapter explains how these representations are learned and what they capture.

The theoretical foundation is the distributional hypothesis (Firth, 1957): 'you shall know a word by the company it keeps.' Words appearing in similar contexts have similar meanings вҖ” 'dog' and 'cat' both appear near 'pet', 'feed', 'vet'. This enables entirely unsupervised learning: no labels, just raw text. Classical n-gram models estimated $p(w_t|w_{t-1},\ldots,w_{t-n+1})$ by counting co-occurrences, but face a fundamental sparsity problem: with a vocabulary of 50,000 words, a 5-gram model has $50000^4 \approx 10^{18}$ possible contexts, most never observed. 'The cat sat on the mat' and 'The dog lay on the floor' share zero 3-grams despite similar meaning вҖ” n-grams cannot generalise across similar words.

Bengio's neural language model (2003) solved this by representing words as learned real-valued vectors. Given these embeddings, similar words have similar contexts, and the model can generalise: even if 'The dog lay' never appeared in training, the similarity of 'dog'/'cat' and 'lay'/'sat' in embedding space allows probability estimation. Word2Vec (Mikolov et al., 2013) simplified this to a dedicated embedding-learning objective. Skip-gram: given centre word $w_t$, predict context words in window $[-k,k]$ by maximising $\sum_t\sum_{j}\log p(w_{t+j}|w_t)$ where $p(w_O|w_I) = \exp(u_{w_O}^\top v_{w_I})/\sum_w\exp(u_w^\top v_{w_I})$. CBOW: predict centre from context (average of context embeddings). Computing the softmax denominator over the full vocabulary is expensive; negative sampling approximates it by distinguishing real pairs from $K\approx5$вҖ“$20$ randomly sampled 'noise' pairs, reducing per-step cost from $O(|V|)$ to $O(K)$.

Well-trained embeddings exhibit remarkable algebraic structure: $v(\text{king}) - v(\text{man}) + v(\text{woman}) \approx v(\text{queen})$. The 'royalty' direction in embedding space ($v(\text{king}) - v(\text{man})$) is approximately parallel to the 'female royalty' direction вҖ” emerging purely from predicting co-occurrences, with no explicit semantic supervision. Similarly, $v(\text{Paris}) - v(\text{France}) + v(\text{Germany}) \approx v(\text{Berlin})$ (capital-country offset), and past-tense offsets are consistent across verbs. GloVe (Pennington, 2014) achieves similar results by directly factorising the global log co-occurrence matrix: $w_i^\top\tilde{w}_j + b_i + \tilde{b}_j = \log X_{ij}$. GloVe has faster convergence by encoding global statistics directly; Word2Vec scales better with corpus size.

These are static embeddings: one vector per word regardless of context. 'Bank' has the same embedding in 'river bank' and 'bank loan'. Contextual embeddings (ELMo, BERT, GPT вҖ” chapter 12) assign different vectors depending on context, capturing polysemy and long-range dependencies. Static embeddings remain useful as efficient baselines, in low-resource settings, and as the conceptual stepping stone to understanding Transformer-based models.
overviews.ch11:
Many real-world problems involve ordered sequences вҖ” text, audio, time series, genomic data. Unlike fixed-size vectors, sequences have variable length and position-dependent structure. RNNs address this by processing sequences step by step, maintaining a hidden state that accumulates context. Weight sharing across time makes them parameter-efficient and allows generalisation across positions.

The core RNN update $h_t = \sigma(W_{xh}x_t + W_{hh}h_{t-1} + b_h)$ combines the current input with the previous hidden state using the same matrices at every step вҖ” analogous to how CNNs share filters across space. The hidden state $h_t \in \mathbb{R}^H$ is a learned compressed representation of all past inputs $x_1,\ldots,x_t$. Its capacity is a strict bottleneck: for short sequences (5вҖ“15 tokens) this works; for long sequences, early information is progressively overwritten by new inputs. This information bottleneck motivates LSTM, GRU, and ultimately Transformers. RNNs support diverse input/output configurations: many-to-one (sentiment analysis вҖ” read the whole review, output one label), one-to-many (image captioning вҖ” input one image, generate many words), and many-to-many (translation вҖ” both encoder and decoder are sequences).

Training uses Backpropagation Through Time (BPTT): unroll the RNN for $T$ steps and apply backpropagation. The gradient of a loss at step $T$ with respect to hidden state $h_k$ requires the product $\prod_{i=k+1}^T \frac{\partial h_i}{\partial h_{i-1}} = \prod_{i=k+1}^T W_{hh}^\top \cdot \text{diag}(\sigma'(z_i))$. Each factor involves $W_{hh}$ (magnification) and sigmoid derivative $\sigma' \leq 0.25$ (attenuation). If the product $< 1$ at each step, gradients vanish exponentially вҖ” for $T=50$ steps and $\|W_{hh}\|\cdot\sigma' = 0.5$: gradient $\approx 0.5^{50} \approx 10^{-15}$. The network effectively cannot learn what happened 20+ steps ago. If the product $> 1$, gradients explode to infinity; this is easier to fix with gradient clipping.

LSTMs (Hochreiter & Schmidhuber, 1997) introduce a cell state $C_t$ running through the sequence with minimal modification. Four gates control information: forget gate $f_t = \sigma(W_f[h_{t-1},x_t])$ decides what to erase from memory; input gate $i_t$ decides what new information to write; candidate $g_t = \tanh(\cdot)$ provides new content; output gate $o_t$ controls what memory to expose as $h_t$. Cell update: $C_t = f_t \odot C_{t-1} + i_t \odot g_t$. The gradient through the cell state is $\partial C_t/\partial C_{t-1} = f_t$ вҖ” when $f_t \approx 1$, gradients flow without attenuation: a gradient highway. LSTMs reliably remember across 100вҖ“1000 steps. GRUs (Cho et al., 2014) simplify LSTMs to two gates (update and reset), merging cell and hidden state, with fewer parameters and similar empirical performance.

For sequence-to-sequence problems (translation, summarisation), an encoder compresses the input into a fixed vector, which the decoder reads to generate the output. This information bottleneck fails for long sequences. Attention (Bahdanau, 2015) solves it: at each decoding step $i$, compute alignment scores $e_{ij} = f_{\text{att}}(s_{i-1}, h_j)$ between the decoder state and each encoder hidden state $h_j$, normalise to attention weights $\alpha_{ij}$, and form a dynamic context vector $c_i = \sum_j\alpha_{ij}h_j$. The decoder can now focus on the relevant part of the input at each step. Visualising $\alpha_{ij}$ reveals interpretable alignment: to generate the French word 'chat', the decoder attends to the English word 'cat'. This mechanism is the direct conceptual precursor to Transformers: chapter 12 removes the RNN entirely and uses self-attention as the primary modelling mechanism.
overviews.ch12:
Transformers (Vaswani et al., 2017) are the architecture that replaced RNNs for sequence modelling and subsequently conquered computer vision, protein structure prediction, and scientific computing. Their key innovation is replacing sequential state-passing (RNNs) with a mechanism that processes entire sequences in parallel: self-attention. Every position attends directly to every other position in a single step, giving $O(1)$ maximum dependency path length (vs $O(n)$ for RNNs) and full GPU parallelism during training.

Self-attention works by projecting the input $X \in \mathbb{R}^{n\times d}$ into three matrices via learned linear projections: queries $Q = XW_Q$, keys $K = XW_K$, values $V = XW_V$. The output is $\text{Attention}(Q,K,V) = \text{softmax}(QK^\top/\sqrt{d_k})V$. Think of this as a differentiable database lookup: each position $i$ uses its query $Q_i$ to measure compatibility with every key $K_j$ (via dot product), applies softmax to get a probability distribution over positions, then takes a weighted sum of values. The $\sqrt{d_k}$ scaling prevents dot products from growing too large (which would push softmax to near-one-hot distributions with vanishing gradients). Since every position can directly access every other, long-range dependencies are learned as easily as short-range ones вҖ” unlike RNNs where distant information must propagate through many state updates.

Self-attention is permutation-equivariant: it has no notion of which position came first. Without positional information, 'cat bites dog' and 'dog bites cat' would have identical representations вҖ” catastrophic for language. Sinusoidal positional encodings inject unique position fingerprints by adding $\text{PE}(\text{pos},2i) = \sin(\text{pos}/10000^{2i/d})$ to each token embedding. Different frequencies across dimensions create a unique code for each position, and $\text{PE}(\text{pos}+k)$ is a linear function of $\text{PE}(\text{pos})$, allowing the model to learn relative positions. Multi-head attention runs $h$ parallel attention computations: $\text{MultiHead} = \text{Concat}(\text{head}_1,\ldots,\text{head}_h)W^O$ where each head has independent projections. With $h=8$ heads, $d=512$, each head operates in a 64-dimensional subspace, learning different relationship types simultaneously (syntactic dependencies, semantic roles, local co-occurrence).

The full Transformer stacks $N$ encoder blocks, each with: multi-head self-attention (any position to any position) followed by a position-wise feed-forward network (two-layer MLP applied independently per position). Both sub-layers have residual connections and Layer Norm: $x \leftarrow \text{LayerNorm}(x + F(x))$. Residuals prevent vanishing gradients in depth; Layer Norm (across features, not batch) stabilises variable-length sequences. The decoder adds masked causal self-attention (position $i$ only attends to $j\leq i$) and cross-attention (queries from decoder, keys/values from encoder output), enabling encoder-decoder translation models.

Pre-training on massive unlabeled text produces representations that transfer to virtually any NLP task with minimal fine-tuning. BERT (encoder-only, 2018) pre-trains by predicting 15% masked tokens using bidirectional context вҖ” ideal for understanding tasks. GPT (decoder-only, 2018вҖ“) pre-trains by predicting the next token: $\mathcal{L}(\theta) = \sum_t \log p(x_t|x_1,\ldots,x_{t-1};\theta)$. This deceptively simple objective, applied to billions of tokens, forces the model to internalise syntax, semantics, factual knowledge, and reasoning вҖ” because all are needed to predict the next word accurately. The representations transfer to classification, QA, translation, and code generation with zero or few labeled examples. Scaling laws (Kaplan et al., 2020) show loss follows power laws in model size, data, and compute вҖ” motivating the investment in ever-larger models. GPT-3 (175B parameters) exhibits emergent few-shot capabilities not present in smaller models. Transformers now dominate not just NLP but vision (ViT), protein folding (AlphaFold2), audio (Whisper), and reinforcement learning.

Lab baДҹlantДұsДұ вҖ” Lab Session 8 (Transformer): PyTorch ile tam Transformer implementasyonu вҖ” embedding + positional encoding, multi-head self-attention, encoder stack, decoder stack (self-attention + cross-attention), feedforward katmanlar. Neural machine translation gГ¶revi Гјzerinde uГ§tan uca eДҹitim, greedy decoding ile autoregressive inference.
overviews.ida01:
Image Data Analysis begins where perception begins: with the digital image itself. Before any algorithm can detect objects, segment cells, or reconstruct three-dimensional scenes, it must grapple with how the physical world is encoded as discrete numbers. A digital image is formally a function $I: \mathbb{Z}^2 \to \{0, \ldots, 2^k-1\}$ mapping pixel coordinates $(x,y)$ to intensity values. For an 8-bit image, $k=8$ and each pixel takes one of 256 possible values. The step from continuous light to discrete numbers involves two separate processes: spatial sampling (how finely is the image grid?) and intensity quantisation (how many bits per pixel?). The NyquistвҖ“Shannon sampling theorem provides the theoretical lower bound вҖ” the sampling frequency must be at least twice the highest spatial frequency in the scene to avoid aliasing. Aliasing manifests as moirГ© patterns and jagged diagonal edges, and is the reason cameras apply an optical anti-aliasing filter before the sensor.

Colour introduces a second representation challenge. The RGB colour space encodes light as additive mixtures of red, green, and blue channels вҖ” physically natural for display hardware, but perceptually non-uniform (equal RGB differences are not equally visible) and inconvenient for analysis (changing illumination rotates RGB values in all three channels simultaneously). The HSV colour space factorises colour into hue (the colour), saturation (the purity), and value (the brightness), making illumination-robust colour segmentation straightforward: threshold on hue while ignoring value. The LAB colour space is the gold standard for perceptual uniformity: a Euclidean distance $\Delta E$ in LAB matches perceived colour difference. These three representations вҖ” RGB, HSV, LAB вҖ” are the foundational vocabulary used in every subsequent chapter when images must be analysed or compared.

The histogram $h(v) = \#\{(x,y) : I(x,y) = v\}$ is the simplest global image statistic: it counts how many pixels take each intensity value, discarding all spatial information. Despite its simplicity, the histogram is remarkably informative: a bimodal histogram reveals a foreground-background structure ripe for thresholding (a concept developed into Otsu's method in chapter ida05); a heavily skewed histogram signals poor contrast. Histogram equalisation corrects contrast by redistributing intensities so that the cumulative distribution function (CDF) becomes approximately uniform. The equalisation mapping is $T(v) = (L-1) \cdot \text{CDF}(v)$, where $L$ is the number of intensity levels. Applied globally, equalisation can over-amplify noise; CLAHE (Contrast Limited Adaptive Histogram Equalisation) applies it locally in tiles with a clip limit, producing far more useful results in medical imaging contexts encountered in later chapters.

Connectivity defines what it means for two pixels to be neighbours, and is the foundation for all region-based analysis. Under 4-connectivity, a pixel at $(x,y)$ has neighbours directly above, below, left, and right вҖ” four in total. Under 8-connectivity, the four diagonal neighbours are added, giving eight total. The choice matters: a thin diagonal line may be 8-connected but not 4-connected, and the boundary of a 4-connected region is 8-connected and vice versa вҖ” a topological duality. Connected-component labelling assigns a unique integer label to each maximal connected region using flood fill, enabling object counting, centroid computation, and region-of-interest extraction. This operation appears in every segmentation pipeline discussed in chapter ida05.

Morphological operations act on binary images via a structuring element $B$ вҖ” a small binary shape (disk, cross, square) that defines the neighbourhood of interaction. Dilation $A \oplus B = \{z : B_z \cap A \neq \emptyset\}$ grows regions outward; erosion $A \ominus B = \{z : B_z \subseteq A\}$ shrinks them. These two operations chain into opening ($A \circ B = (A \ominus B) \oplus B$, erosion then dilation) which removes small objects and smooths boundaries, and closing ($A \bullet B = (A \oplus B) \ominus B$, dilation then erosion) which fills small holes. Opening and closing are idempotent вҖ” applying them twice gives the same result as once вҖ” a useful algebraic property. These elementary operations remain in use throughout the course: morphological watersheds underpin classical segmentation (ida05), and the structuring element foreshadows the convolution kernel introduced systematically in ida02.
overviews.ida02:
If chapter ida01 establishes what an image is, chapter ida02 establishes the fundamental operations that transform images. Three mathematical frameworks are developed in parallel вҖ” spatial filtering via convolution, structural analysis via morphology, and frequency-domain analysis via the Fourier transform вҖ” and understanding their interrelationships is central to everything that follows in the course.

Discrete convolution is defined as $(I * h)[x,y] = \sum_{u,v} I[x-u, y-v]\, h[u,v]$, where $h$ is the filter kernel. The sign flip in the indices (correlation uses $+u,+v$; convolution uses $-u,-v$) matters when the kernel is not symmetric, but is irrelevant for symmetric kernels like Gaussians. The Gaussian filter $G(x,y;\sigma) = \frac{1}{2\pi\sigma^2}\exp\!\left(-\frac{x^2+y^2}{2\sigma^2}\right)$ is separable: $G(x,y;\sigma) = G(x;\sigma)\cdot G(y;\sigma)$. Separability enables a $M\times N$ convolution with a $k\times k$ kernel in $O(MNk)$ operations instead of $O(MNk^2)$ вҖ” a factor of $k$ reduction that matters enormously for large $k$. Gaussian smoothing at scale $\sigma$ removes structures finer than $\approx 2\sigma$ pixels, making it the canonical scale-space operator. The Laplacian of Gaussian (LoG) $\nabla^2 G$ combines smoothing with second-derivative sharpening: it detects blobs and edges as zero-crossings, and is equivalent to the difference of two Gaussians (DoG) at slightly different scales вҖ” a fact exploited by SIFT (chapter ida13).

Edge detection exemplifies the signal-processing perspective on images. The Sobel operator approximates the first spatial derivative: $\nabla I \approx (I * S_x, I * S_y)$ where $S_x$ and $S_y$ are $3\times3$ antisymmetric kernels. The gradient magnitude $|\nabla I| = \sqrt{(I*S_x)^2 + (I*S_y)^2}$ peaks at edges; the gradient direction $\theta = \arctan\!\left(\frac{I*S_y}{I*S_x}\right)$ indicates the edge orientation. The Canny detector (1986) adds two refinements: non-maximum suppression thins wide ridges to single-pixel edges by keeping only local maxima in the gradient direction; hysteresis thresholding uses two thresholds $T_{\text{low}}$ and $T_{\text{high}}$ to accept edge pixels вҖ” strong above $T_{\text{high}}$, weak only if connected to a strong pixel. This produces thin, connected edges robust to noise, and remains one of the most widely used preprocessing steps despite being over 35 years old.

The morphological operations introduced in ida01 receive their rigorous algebraic treatment here. Erosion and dilation are dual under complement: $\overline{A \oplus B} = \bar{A} \ominus B$, meaning dilation of the foreground equals erosion of the background. The hit-or-miss transform detects specific shapes by simultaneously eroding with a foreground pattern and eroding the complement with a background pattern, returning only pixels where both match simultaneously. Morphological gradient $A \oplus B - A \ominus B$ outlines object boundaries. These operations are exact and interpretable вҖ” important in scientific imaging contexts where a pixel-level explanation of every decision is required, as seen in the biological segmentation methods of chapter ida05.

The Discrete Fourier Transform (DFT) decomposes an image into complex sinusoids: $F(u,v) = \sum_{x=0}^{M-1}\sum_{y=0}^{N-1} I(x,y)\exp\!\left(-2\pi i\left(\frac{ux}{M}+\frac{vy}{N}\right)\right)$. The central coefficient $F(0,0)$ is the mean intensity (the DC component); coefficients at large $(u,v)$ capture fine spatial detail (high frequencies). The convolution theorem is the DFT's most powerful result: $\mathcal{F}\{I*h\} = \mathcal{F}\{I\} \cdot \mathcal{F}\{h\}$. Convolution in the spatial domain becomes pointwise multiplication in the frequency domain. For large kernels, it is faster to compute the DFT of both, multiply, and inverse-transform than to convolve directly. Frequency-domain analysis also makes the physics of filtering transparent: a low-pass filter (retain small $(u,v)$) blurs the image; a high-pass filter (retain large $(u,v)$) reveals edges; a notch filter removes specific spatial frequencies such as scan-line artefacts in microscopy. Truncating the frequency spectrum before inverse transform introduces Gibbs phenomenon (ringing near sharp edges) вҖ” the price paid for abrupt spectral cuts. The DFT perspective directly motivates why CNNs (ida03, ML ch09) learn to behave as adaptive band-pass filter banks.

Exercise baДҹlantДұsДұ вҖ” Exercise 02: Bu chapter'Дұn pratik uygulamasДұ. 3D floresan mikroskobi gГ¶rГјntГјsГј (Arabidopsis thaliana hГјcre zarДұ) Гјzerinde Г¶nce gamma dГјzeltmesi ile kontrast artДұrДұmДұ, ardДұndan median filtre ile gГјrГјltГј temizleme (zarlarДұ koruyarak), Gaussian filtre ve separable filter denkliДҹinin doДҹrulanmasДұ. Frekans alanДұnda FFT + ball Еҹeklinde low-pass filtre uygulamasДұ. Son olarak morphological erosion ile instance segmentation maskДұndan hГјcre zarДұ sДұnДұrlarДұnДұ Г§Дұkarma.
overviews.ida03:
Chapters ida01 and ida02 develop classical, hand-engineered image analysis tools. Chapter ida03 introduces the data-driven paradigm that has superseded most of them: deep learning. Rather than deriving a specific algorithm for each image task, a neural network is trained end-to-end to learn the appropriate transformation directly from data. The chapter serves as a self-contained crash course, covering the same material as ML chapters ch07 (neural networks), ch08 (optimisation), and ch09 (CNNs) but with an emphasis on the image-specific design choices that make deep models work.

The multilayer perceptron (MLP) begins from the perceptron: a single linear threshold unit $\hat{y} = \text{sign}(w^\top x + b)$. The XOR problem proved that no single hyperplane can classify all configurations, motivating hidden layers: with one hidden layer computing intermediate features, the input space is non-linearly remapped so that a final linear layer can separate any configuration. The formal MLP update rule is $a^{(l)} = g\!\left(W^{(l)} a^{(l-1)} + b^{(l)}\right)$, where $g$ is the elementwise activation function. Without $g$, the composition of linear layers collapses to a single linear transformation вҖ” depth becomes meaningless. The Universal Approximation Theorem guarantees that one hidden layer suffices to approximate any continuous function, but the exponential number of neurons required motivates the use of many layers with polynomial parameter counts. ReLU $\max(0,z)$ is the standard activation: its gradient is 1 for $z>0$ and 0 otherwise, preventing gradient attenuation for positive values and enabling training of very deep networks.

Backpropagation (Rumelhart et al., 1986) computes the gradient of the scalar loss with respect to every parameter in a single backward pass. Define error signals $\delta^{(l)} = \partial \mathcal{L}/\partial z^{(l)}$. At the output layer: $\delta^{(L)} = \frac{\partial\mathcal{L}}{\partial a^{(L)}} \odot g'(z^{(L)})$. For each hidden layer: $\delta^{(l)} = \left(W^{(l+1)}\right)^\top \delta^{(l+1)} \odot g'(z^{(l)})$. Weight gradients: $\partial\mathcal{L}/\partial W^{(l)} = \delta^{(l)}(a^{(l-1)})^\top$. The key property is that computing all gradients costs $O(1)$ backward passes regardless of the number of parameters вҖ” not one pass per parameter вҖ” making training feasible for models with millions of weights. Vanishing gradients in deep networks (products of weight matrices and activation derivatives shrinking exponentially) are mitigated by ReLU activations, batch normalisation, and residual connections.

Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) are the canonical architecture for image data, introduced in ML ch09 and applied throughout this course. Local receptive fields (each unit sees only a small patch), weight sharing (the same filter slides over the entire image), and pooling (local aggregation for translation invariance) together reduce parameters by orders of magnitude compared to fully-connected layers while encoding spatial inductive biases. A convolutional layer with $C_{\text{out}}$ filters of size $k\times k$ on input with $C_{\text{in}}$ channels has $k^2 C_{\text{in}} C_{\text{out}}$ parameters вҖ” independent of image size. Stacking convolutional layers, non-linearities, and pooling builds a hierarchy: early layers detect edges and textures; deep layers respond to semantic categories. This hierarchical feature learning is precisely what the subsequent detection and segmentation architectures (ida04, ida05) exploit.

The loss function and output activation must be jointly designed for the task. For regression (e.g., bounding box coordinates), a linear output with MSE loss $\mathcal{L} = \|y - \hat{y}\|^2$ is appropriate. For binary classification (foreground/background in segmentation), a sigmoid output $\hat{p} = \sigma(z)$ with binary cross-entropy $\mathcal{L} = -[t\log\hat{p} + (1-t)\log(1-\hat{p})]$ is standard. For $K$-class classification, softmax outputs $\hat{p}_k = \exp(z_k)/\sum_j\exp(z_j)$ with categorical cross-entropy. A critical design for imbalanced datasets вҖ” prevalent in detection where most locations are background вҖ” is the focal loss $\text{FL}(p_t) = -(1-p_t)^\gamma \log(p_t)$, which down-weights well-classified easy examples by factor $(1-p_t)^\gamma$ (typically $\gamma=2$), forcing the model to concentrate on the rare hard positives. Focal loss is the enabling component of RetinaNet and dense object detectors discussed in chapter ida04.

Exercise baДҹlantДұsДұ вҖ” Exercise 03: Bu chapter'Дұn uygulamalДұ karЕҹДұlДұДҹДұ. Perceptron ile AND/OR mantДұk kapДұlarДұnДұ sДұfДұrdan implement etme ve lineer ayrДұlabilirlik sДұnДұrДұnДұ gГ¶rme. MLP ile sinГјs fonksiyonu tahmini (Keras, farklДұ layer derinlikleri karЕҹДұlaЕҹtДұrmasДұ). LeNet benzeri CNN ile MNIST rakam sДұnДұflandДұrmasДұ вҖ” %98.6 test accuracy, Г¶Дҹrenilen kernel'larДұn gГ¶rselleЕҹtirilmesi ve katmanlar arasДұ feature map analizi.
overviews.ida04:
Object detection asks not just 'what is in this image?' but 'where, and how many?'. This extension from classification to localisation constitutes one of the most impactful problems in computer vision, with direct applications in autonomous driving, medical image analysis, surveillance, and robotics. The chapter traces the evolution from classical sliding-window approaches to modern single-pass deep detectors, each transition motivated by a specific bottleneck in speed or accuracy.

Classical detection relied on template matching вҖ” scanning a reference patch over the image and finding locations of high correlation. This is conceptually equivalent to a single convolution (ida02 convolution theorem) but fails for objects with intra-class variability (different instances of 'car' look very different). Sliding-window detectors overcome this by training a strong binary classifier (an SVM with HOG features, or the Viola-Jones cascade for faces) and applying it exhaustively at multiple scales and aspect ratios. The Viola-Jones detector introduced two critical ideas: integral images for $O(1)$ feature computation at any scale, and cascaded classifiers that reject most windows at the first stage and only devote computation to promising candidates. Despite impressive real-time face detection performance, the hand-engineered feature pipeline fundamentally limits generalisation.

R-CNN (Girshick et al., 2014) replaced hand-crafted features with deep CNN representations. The pipeline is: (1) selective search generates $\approx2000$ region proposals per image based on colour and texture similarity; (2) each proposal is warped to $227\times227$ and forward-passed through a CNN independently; (3) the resulting features feed an SVM classifier and a bounding-box regressor. The accuracy was transformative; the speed was not вҖ” 47 seconds per image on a GPU, because $2000$ independent CNN passes are required. Fast R-CNN (2015) fixed the efficiency bottleneck by inverting the order of operations: run the CNN once on the whole image to get a shared feature map, then extract per-region features with RoI Pooling вҖ” a spatial max-pooling operation that maps any-sized region to a fixed $7\times7$ grid by dividing the region into a $7\times7$ grid and max-pooling within each cell. This reduced inference to $\approx2$ seconds per image, with the bottleneck now in selective search proposal generation.

Faster R-CNN (2015) eliminated selective search entirely by introducing the Region Proposal Network (RPN). The RPN slides a small network over the convolutional feature map and, at each spatial location, predicts objectness scores and bounding-box offsets for $k$ anchor boxes вҖ” predefined boxes of different scales and aspect ratios centred at that location. Anchors with IoU $>0.7$ with a ground-truth box are positive; IoU $<0.3$ are negative. The RPN and the detection head share the convolutional backbone, making the entire system trainable end-to-end. Inference requires $\approx0.2$ seconds per image вҖ” real-time for many applications. Faster R-CNN remains a standard two-stage baseline: high accuracy at the cost of two sequential networks.

YOLO (You Only Look Once, Redmon et al., 2016) reformulated detection as a single regression problem. The image is divided into an $S\times S$ grid (e.g., $7\times7$); each cell predicts $B$ bounding boxes parameterised as $(x,y,w,h,\text{confidence})$ and $C$ class probability vectors. Confidence is $P(\text{object}) \times \text{IoU}_{\text{pred,truth}}$. All predictions are made in a single forward pass, enabling 45 FPS detection on a GPU. The price is reduced accuracy on small objects and objects whose centres fall in the same cell. Feature Pyramid Networks (FPN, Lin et al., 2017) address multi-scale detection by adding a top-down pathway with lateral connections to the standard bottom-up CNN backbone. Each resolution level in the pyramid receives both fine-grained spatial information from the bottom-up path and rich semantic information from the top-down path, enabling accurate detection of objects across a wide range of scales. FPN is now a standard component in virtually all modern detectors. The focal loss introduced in ida03 was specifically designed for RetinaNet, a single-stage detector with FPN that achieves two-stage accuracy at single-stage speed вҖ” resolving the long-standing speed-accuracy trade-off.

Exercise baДҹlantДұsДұ вҖ” Exercise 04: Д°ki ayrДұ gГ¶rev. Д°lk kДұsДұmda PyTorch'ta pretrained ResNet50-FPN backbone'lu Faster R-CNN ile inference вҖ” proposal kutularДұ ve final tespitlerin gГ¶rselleЕҹtirilmesi. Д°kinci kДұsДұmda sДұfДұrdan YOLO-tarzДұ tek aЕҹamalДұ hГјcre dedektГ¶rГј: Human U2OS hГјcreleri (BBBC006, public dataset), 32Г—32 grid tabanlДұ Г§ДұktДұ, score/offset/size iГ§in ayrДұ FC head'ler, ResNet50 backbone, 50 epoch eДҹitim.
overviews.ida05:
Detection (ida04) answers 'where is the object?' at bounding-box granularity. Segmentation goes further: it answers 'which pixel belongs to which object?' вҖ” a dense prediction problem requiring a label for every one of the $M\times N$ pixels in the image. This is both technically harder and scientifically more valuable: in biology, exact cell boundaries determine morphological measurements; in autonomous driving, knowing the precise road boundary is safety-critical.

Classical segmentation begins with global thresholding, the simplest possible region assignment. Otsu's method (1979) finds the threshold $T^*$ that minimises the weighted within-class variance of the two resulting regions, equivalent to maximising between-class variance. It derives $T^*$ analytically from the histogram вҖ” an $O(L)$ computation regardless of image size. Otsu assumes a bimodal intensity distribution; when this fails (uneven illumination, multiple objects), adaptive thresholding and more sophisticated methods are needed. The watershed algorithm treats the image gradient magnitude as a topographic surface: water poured into local minima rises and eventually meets water from adjacent basins, forming watershed lines вҖ” the segmentation boundaries. Watershed produces closed contours and handles complex topology but is sensitive to noise-induced local minima, requiring marker-controlled variants. Graph cuts formulate segmentation as a minimum-cut problem: pixels are graph nodes, edge weights encode colour similarity, and finding the minimum cut between source (foreground) and sink (background) terminals gives the globally optimal binary segmentation under the energy model вҖ” tractable via max-flow algorithms.

U-Net (Ronneberger et al., 2015) is the architecture that transformed biomedical image segmentation. The motivation was specific: biomedical datasets contain tens to hundreds of images rather than millions; the network must localise precisely (no image-level labels); and cell boundaries require fine-grained spatial resolution. U-Net's encoder-decoder architecture with skip connections addresses all three simultaneously. The encoder (contracting path) applies repeated $3\times3$ convolutions with $2\times2$ max-pooling, doubling channels at each level while halving spatial resolution вҖ” standard CNN feature extraction. The decoder (expansive path) upsamples via transposed convolutions, but critically, concatenates the corresponding encoder feature map via skip connections at each resolution level. These skip connections provide the decoder with precise spatial information that was lost during downsampling. The input is $572\times572$ but the output is $388\times388$ (valid convolutions, not padded) вҖ” the prediction covers only the central region to avoid boundary artefacts. Trained with aggressive data augmentation (elastic deformations, rotations, flips), U-Net achieves state-of-the-art results with as few as 30 training images.

Mask R-CNN (He et al., 2017) extends Faster R-CNN to instance segmentation: distinguishing between individual object instances, not just semantic categories. The extension adds a third head (alongside classification and bounding-box regression) that predicts a binary mask for each detected region. The critical technical contribution is RoI Align, which replaces the quantised RoI Pooling of Fast R-CNN with bilinear interpolation вҖ” avoiding the integer rounding errors that misalign features by up to 0.5 pixels. For $28\times28$ mask prediction, this misalignment is devastating; RoI Align eliminates it at negligible computational cost, substantially improving mask accuracy. Mask R-CNN achieves state-of-the-art instance segmentation and serves as the baseline for the biology-specific methods below.

Segmentation metrics are essential for model evaluation and comparison. Intersection over Union $\text{IoU} = |A\cap B|/|A\cup B|$ measures overlap between predicted and ground-truth regions; the Dice coefficient $\text{Dice} = 2|A\cap B|/(|A|+|B|)$ is mathematically equivalent to the F1-score and more sensitive near zero-overlap. Cellpose (Stringer et al., 2021) takes a fundamentally different approach for biological cell segmentation: instead of predicting masks directly, it predicts a gradient flow field pointing from every pixel toward the centre of its owning cell. Following these gradients by simulation groups pixels into cells вҖ” the method handles cells of arbitrary shape including elongated, branching, and densely packed morphologies that confound mask-based approaches. StarDist predicts for each pixel a set of radial distances to the cell boundary in $n$ directions, fitting a star-convex polygon вҖ” a shape that is the most natural representation for typical round/oval cell shapes in fluorescence microscopy. Both Cellpose and StarDist illustrate how domain knowledge of object geometry can be incorporated into neural network output parameterisations rather than treated as post-processing.

Exercise baДҹlantДұsДұ вҖ” Exercise 05: StarDist ve Cellpose ile pratik hГјcre segmentasyonu. StarDist kДұsmДұnda 4вҖ“128 vertex arasДұnda polygon temsil doДҹruluДҹu karЕҹДұlaЕҹtДұrmasДұ (32'nin neden optimal olduДҹunun gГ¶rГјlmesi), ardДұndan pretrained 2D_demo modeliyle test gГ¶rГјntГјlerinde inference ve polygon gГ¶rselleЕҹtirme. Cellpose kДұsmДұnda pretrained cyto modeliyle vertikal/horizontal gradient ve semantic segmentation Г§ДұktДұlarДұnДұn incelenmesi, gradient tracking ile final instance maskelerinin Гјretilmesi.

Lab baДҹlantДұsДұ вҖ” Lab Session 5 (CNN for Segmentation): Pascal VOC segmentation dataset Гјzerinde semantic segmentation. VGG'nin fully connected katmanlarДұnДұ Conv'a dГ¶nГјЕҹtГјrerek FCN yapma, transposed convolution ile spatial boyutu geri kazanma, skip connection ile farklДұ Г¶lГ§eklerden feature birleЕҹtirme. mIoU ve Dice score ile deДҹerlendirme. Bonus olarak tam U-Net encoder-decoder mimarisi. Bu lab'Дұ IDA exercise 05'teki instance segmentation (StarDist, Cellpose) ile birlikte dГјЕҹГјnГјnce semantic вҶ’ instance segmentation farkДұ daha net oturuyor.
overviews.ida06:
Detection and segmentation (ida04, ida05) operate on individual frames. Many applications вҖ” autonomous driving, multi-object sports analysis, biological cell migration вҖ” require tracking objects continuously through time, associating detections across frames and maintaining persistent identities even through occlusions and re-entries. This chapter covers three complementary techniques: probabilistic state estimation with the Kalman filter, combinatorial assignment with the Hungarian algorithm, and dense motion estimation with optical flow.

The Kalman filter (Kalman, 1960) is the optimal linear estimator for a state-space model subject to Gaussian noise. The system model defines state dynamics: $\hat{x}_k = A\hat{x}_{k-1} + B u_k$ (prediction step), where $A$ is the transition matrix (e.g., constant-velocity motion: $A$ integrates velocity into position), $B u_k$ is optional control input, and prediction uncertainty grows as $P_k = A P_{k-1} A^\top + Q$ where $Q$ is the process noise covariance. The update step incorporates a new measurement $z_k$: compute the Kalman gain $K = P_k H^\top (H P_k H^\top + R)^{-1}$ where $H$ maps state to measurement space and $R$ is measurement noise covariance. The state and covariance are then corrected: $\hat{x}_k \leftarrow \hat{x}_k + K(z_k - H\hat{x}_k)$ and $P_k \leftarrow (I - KH)P_k$. The Kalman gain automatically balances trust in the prediction (shaped by $Q$) against trust in the measurement (shaped by $R$): high $R$ (noisy measurements) means trust the prediction more; high $Q$ (uncertain dynamics) means trust the measurement more. For non-linear dynamics (turning cars, swimming cells), the Extended Kalman Filter linearises around the current state; the Unscented Kalman Filter uses deterministic sigma points to propagate the full distribution non-linearly.

In multi-object tracking, detections from frame $t$ must be assigned to existing tracks from frame $t-1$. This is the assignment problem: given a cost matrix $C_{ij}$ (e.g., IoU between predicted track positions and detected bounding boxes), find the one-to-one assignment minimising total cost. The Hungarian algorithm (Kuhn, 1955; Munkres, 1957) solves this in $O(n^3)$ time, guaranteed globally optimal. The SORT algorithm (Simple Online and Realtime Tracking, 2016) combines Kalman prediction for bounding box state estimation with Hungarian assignment on IoU costs вҖ” achieving real-time multi-object tracking with minimal implementation complexity. DeepSORT extends SORT by incorporating appearance features from a deep CNN alongside IoU costs, enabling re-identification of objects returning after long occlusions where IoU-only cost fails. A track is terminated if unmatched for more than $T_{\text{lost}}$ frames and initialised for any unmatched detection.

Optical flow estimates the dense velocity field $\mathbf{v}(x,y) = (u,v)$ describing how each pixel moves between two consecutive frames. The brightness constancy assumption вҖ” $I(x,y,t) = I(x+u, y+v, t+1)$ вҖ” combined with first-order Taylor expansion gives the aperture problem equation: $I_x u + I_y v + I_t = 0$, a single equation in two unknowns, under-determined per pixel. Lucas-Kanade (1981) resolves this by assuming constant flow within a small window $\mathcal{W}$ and solving the resulting overdetermined system by least squares: $[u,v]^\top = (A^\top A)^{-1} A^\top b$ where rows of $A$ are $(I_x, I_y)$ for each window pixel. The matrix $A^\top A$ is the Harris structure tensor (also used for corner detection); the flow is reliable only where both eigenvalues are large вҖ” at corners, not along edges. Horn-Schunck (1981) instead imposes global smoothness: minimise $\int (I_x u + I_y v + I_t)^2 + \alpha(|\nabla u|^2+|\nabla v|^2)\,dx\,dy$, producing dense flow fields at the cost of blurring flow boundaries. FlowNet and subsequent deep architectures learn to predict optical flow end-to-end, dramatically improving accuracy on large-displacement and occluded regions. The flow field computed here feeds directly into temporal consistency constraints in video segmentation and action recognition pipelines.

Exercise baДҹlantДұsДұ вҖ” Exercise 06: Mitoz geГ§iren hГјcrelerin 3 frame'lik mikroskopi dizisinde tracking. Naive nearest-neighbor atama (scipy.spatial.distance.cdist) ile centroid bazlДұ takip ve hatalarДұn analizi. Lineage tree ve state space diyagramДұ elle Г§izme. Viterbi algoritmasДұnДұn nasДұl optimal path bulduДҹunun tartДұЕҹДұlmasДұ. Son kДұsДұmda OpenCV Farneback optical flow ile frame'ler arasДұ yoДҹun displacement field hesaplama ve gГ¶rselleЕҹtirme.
overviews.ida07:
Chapters ida03вҖ“ida06 build on CNN-based architectures for every vision task. But CNNs have a structural limitation: their receptive field grows linearly with depth, requiring many layers to integrate information across large spatial distances. Self-attention, the mechanism developed for natural language in ML ch12, computes relationships between all pairs of positions simultaneously вҖ” $O(1)$ maximum path length regardless of image size. Chapter ida07 covers how this mechanism is adapted to vision and why it succeeds where CNNs struggle.

Self-attention (Vaswani et al., 2017; ML ch12) projects an input sequence into query, key, and value matrices: $Q = XW_Q$, $K = XW_K$, $V = XW_V$. The output is $\text{Attention}(Q,K,V) = \text{softmax}(QK^\top/\sqrt{d_k})V$. Each position's output is a weighted sum of all other positions' values, where the weights are dot-product similarities between queries and keys. Multi-head attention runs $h$ parallel attention computations in lower-dimensional subspaces, concatenates, and projects: $\text{MultiHead}(Q,K,V) = \text{Concat}(\text{head}_1,\ldots,\text{head}_h)W^O$. With $h=8$ heads and $d=512$, each head operates in a 64-dimensional space and specialises in different relationship types вҖ” syntactic, semantic, local, global. The critical property for vision is that the global attention map is computed from the very first layer: a pixel in the top-left corner can directly influence the representation of a pixel in the bottom-right corner without waiting for $\log_2(N)$ pooling layers.

The Vision Transformer (ViT, Dosovitskiy et al., 2020) applies the standard Transformer encoder directly to images by serialising them into patches. An image is divided into $16\times16$ non-overlapping patches, each flattened and linearly projected into a $d$-dimensional embedding. A learnable $[\text{CLS}]$ token is prepended; its output representation is used for classification. 1D learnable position embeddings are added to inform the model of patch positions, since self-attention is permutation-invariant. The sequence of $N+1$ tokens passes through $L$ standard Transformer encoder blocks (multi-head self-attention + layer norm + MLP + residual connections). The key finding: ViT matches or exceeds CNN accuracy, but only when pre-trained on very large datasets (ImageNet-21k with 14M images, or JFT-300M). With small datasets, CNNs' inductive biases (locality, translation equivariance) provide a significant advantage; ViT must learn these from data, requiring much more data to reach the same quality. Hybrid architectures prepend a CNN backbone to the Transformer, combining spatial efficiency with global attention.

DETR (DEtection TRansformer, Carion et al., 2020) applies the encoder-decoder Transformer architecture directly to object detection, eliminating the anchor boxes, RPN, and NMS post-processing that define the R-CNN family (ida04). A CNN backbone extracts a feature map; the Transformer encoder processes it with full self-attention; the decoder takes $N$ learnable object queries and attends to the encoder output via cross-attention. Each query produces one detection (class + bounding box), with unused queries predicting 'no object'. Training uses a bipartite matching loss: Hungarian algorithm matches the $N$ predictions to the ground-truth objects (padded to $N$), and only the matched pairs contribute to the loss. This set prediction formulation makes the detector permutation-invariant over predictions вҖ” no ordering is assumed вҖ” and eliminates redundant duplicate detections without NMS. DETR achieves Faster R-CNN accuracy on large objects but underperforms on small objects due to the global nature of encoder attention; this is addressed by Deformable DETR.

Swin Transformer (Liu et al., 2021) combines ViT's attention mechanism with the hierarchical feature pyramid of CNNs. The key innovation is windowed attention: rather than computing attention between all patch pairs (quadratic in image size), Swin restricts attention to non-overlapping local windows of $W\times W$ patches, giving $O(n)$ complexity in image size. Shifted window attention вҖ” alternating between regular and shifted window partitions across layers вҖ” enables cross-window connections without breaking the $O(n)$ complexity. A hierarchical architecture (patch merging at each stage) produces multi-resolution feature maps compatible with FPN-based detection and segmentation heads. Swin achieved state-of-the-art results on ImageNet classification, COCO object detection, and ADE20K semantic segmentation simultaneously, establishing it as the first ViT-based architecture that could serve as a universal vision backbone replacing CNNs.

Exercise baДҹlantДұsДұ вҖ” Exercise 07 + Exercise 08: Exercise 07'nin ikinci yarДұsДұnda TensorFlow'da sДұfДұrdan Multi-Head Attention ve positional encoding implement edilerek IMDB film yorumu sДұnДұflandДұrmasДұ (%86 test accuracy). Exercise 08'de PyTorch'ta DETR (ResNet50 backbone + Transformer encoder/decoder) ile nesne tespiti, encoder self-attention haritasДұnДұn (8Г—850Г—850) 2D spatial yapДұya reshape edilip gГ¶rselleЕҹtirilmesi, Swin Transformer'Дұn shifted window mekanizmasДұ, TrackFormer'Дұn 4 adДұmДұ.
overviews.ida08:
Chapter ida07 established Transformers as competitive with CNNs on standard vision benchmarks. Chapter ida08 pushes further, exploring architectures where the Transformer is not merely an add-on to a CNN backbone but the primary modelling component вҖ” for 3D medical image segmentation, promptable interactive segmentation, and self-supervised pre-training. These systems represent the current frontier of foundation models in visual analysis.

UNETR (Transformers for 3D Medical Image Segmentation, Hatamizadeh et al., 2022) adapts the ViT encoder for volumetric medical imaging. A 3D image volume (e.g., CT scan, $128\times128\times128$ voxels) is divided into $16\times16\times16$ patches, flattened, and linearly projected to form a sequence of tokens for a standard ViT encoder. The key architectural insight is that the Transformer encoder's intermediate representations, extracted at multiple depths, carry information at different semantic levels вҖ” similar to the encoder levels of U-Net. These intermediate tokens are reshaped from sequences back into volumetric feature maps and fed into a CNN decoder with skip connections, following the U-Net structure (ida05). The encoder captures long-range dependencies across the volume via global self-attention вҖ” impossible for CNNs within a single layer вҖ” while the CNN decoder provides precise boundary localisation at high resolution. UNETR substantially outperforms U-Net on complex 3D segmentation tasks, particularly for organs with large shape variability.

The Segment Anything Model (SAM, Kirillov et al., 2023) addresses a fundamentally different question: rather than training a specialised segmentor for one domain, can a single model segment any object in any image given a prompt? SAM introduces the concept of promptable segmentation: the model accepts a point, set of points, bounding box, or text as a specification of 'what to segment', and outputs a corresponding mask. The architecture has three components. The image encoder is a large ViT-H (huge ViT) that processes the input image once and produces a rich image embedding вҖ” this is the expensive step ($\sim90\%$ of compute). The prompt encoder converts geometric prompts (points, boxes) to embeddings using positional encodings, and text prompts via CLIP features. The mask decoder is lightweight: it uses cross-attention between prompt embeddings and image embeddings, followed by a simple MLP, to predict the mask in a few milliseconds. This asymmetry вҖ” expensive image encoder, cheap prompt decoder вҖ” enables interactive segmentation at interactive speeds. SAM was trained on SA-1B, a dataset of over one billion masks collected using a data engine that iteratively used SAM to assist human annotators. SAM achieves zero-shot generalisation to domains far from its training distribution, including microscopy, satellite imagery, and surgical video.

Masked Autoencoders (MAE, He et al., 2022) are a self-supervised pre-training method for ViTs. The core idea: mask 75% of image patches at random, pass only the visible patches through the ViT encoder, then pass the full set (visible + masked tokens) through a lightweight decoder to reconstruct the original pixel values of masked patches. This is asymmetric вҖ” the encoder only processes 25% of patches, making pre-training three times faster. The high masking ratio is critical: with low masking (e.g., 25%), simple interpolation between visible patches suffices; at 75%, the model must learn genuine semantic understanding to reconstruct coherent content. MAE pre-training produces ViT representations that fine-tune to state-of-the-art results on classification, detection, and segmentation with substantially fewer labeled examples than training from scratch.

DINO (self-DIstillation with NO labels, Caron et al., 2021) is a self-supervised method revealing a remarkable emergent property of Transformers: without any labels, the model learns to segment objects. DINO uses a student-teacher framework where both share the same ViT architecture. The student processes small local crops; the teacher (updated as an exponential moving average of the student) processes the full image. A cross-entropy loss between their output distributions (after a centering and sharpening operation to prevent collapse) drives the student to predict the teacher's global representation from local views. The striking result: the self-attention maps of the $[\text{CLS}]$ token in the final layer cleanly segment foreground objects from background, with no segmentation supervision вҖ” purely from the local-to-global consistency objective. This emergent segmentation property is not observed in CNN-based self-supervised methods, suggesting that Transformer attention specifically learns object-centric representations.

Exercise baДҹlantДұsДұ вҖ” Exercise 08: DETR mimarisinin PyTorch implementasyonu ve incelemesi. Pretrained DETR ile inference, encoder attention haritasДұnДұn feature map koordinatlarДұna geri dГ¶nГјЕҹtГјrГјlmesi вҖ” object boundary'lerin bounding box supervision olmadan nasДұl Г¶ДҹrenildiДҹinin gГ¶zlemlenmesi. Swin Transformer local window attention ve cross-window shift mekanizmasДұ sorularДұ. TrackFormer'Дұn CNN backbone вҶ’ Transformer encoder вҶ’ decoder query sistemi akДұЕҹДұ. Notebook'un sonunda SAM (Segment Anything) online demo'ya kДұsa deДҹinme.

Lab baДҹlantДұsДұ вҖ” Lab Session 9 (Anomaly Detection & CLIP): MVTec AD dataset (sanayi ГјrГјnleri, wood kategorisi) Гјzerinde zero-shot anomaly segmentation. OpenAI CLIP modeli yГјkleme, gГ¶rГјntГј patch'lerini CNN encoder ile feature'a Г§evirme, "anomalous texture" / "normal texture" gibi text prompt'larДұnДұ text encoder'dan geГ§irme, patch feature'larДұ ile text embedding'lerin cosine similarity'si ile anomaly heatmap Гјretme. Fine-tuning olmadan sДұfДұr Г¶rnekle Г§alДұЕҹmasДұ CLIP'in vision-language alignment gГјcГјnГј somut gГ¶steriyor.
overviews.ida09:
All preceding chapters work with 2D images вҖ” a natural choice for cameras but not for 3D sensors. LiDAR sensors (in autonomous vehicles) and depth cameras (in robotics, AR/VR, medical imaging) produce point clouds: unordered sets of 3D coordinates $\{(x_i, y_i, z_i)\}_{i=1}^N$, each potentially with colour or intensity attributes. The challenge is fundamental: unlike images, point clouds have no regular grid structure, no fixed neighbourhood, and no canonical ordering. A neural network that receives the points in a different order must produce the same output вҖ” a property called permutation invariance that standard convolutions completely lack.

A point cloud from a LiDAR scan contains $\sim10^5$ points irregularly distributed in 3D space вҖ” dense near the sensor, sparse far away. Early approaches avoided the permutation problem by projecting point clouds onto 2D images (range images, bird's-eye-view) and applying standard CNNs. This discards 3D structure and introduces projection artefacts. Voxelisation discretises 3D space into a grid and applies 3D convolutions вҖ” but the cubic scaling of voxel count with resolution makes fine-grained representation prohibitively expensive. PointNet (Qi et al., 2017) is the first architecture to operate directly on raw point sets, achieving permutation invariance by design.

PointNet processes each point independently with a shared MLP (the same weights applied to every point, analogous to a $1\times1$ convolution in CNNs), then aggregates all point features via global max pooling: $g(\{x_1,\ldots,x_n\}) = \text{MAX}\{h(x_1),\ldots,h(x_n)\}$ where $h$ is the point-wise MLP and MAX is elementwise. Max pooling is symmetric under permutation and differentiable вҖ” backpropagation flows through it correctly. The critical points theorem formalises what the network actually learns: the output of global max pooling is determined by a small subset of the input points (those achieving the maximum in each feature dimension) called critical points. The network can be thought of as detecting a sparse set of characteristic point patterns; the remaining points are redundant. Before the MLP, a T-Net (Input Transform) predicts a $3\times3$ transformation matrix to align the input to a canonical orientation вҖ” making the network invariant to rigid transformations. A second Feature Transform aligns the intermediate feature space. The final architecture achieves strong performance on ModelNet40 classification and ShapeNet part segmentation with a remarkably simple design.

PointNet's critical limitation is the absence of local context: each point is processed independently before pooling. Two points from different objects could produce the same global descriptor if they each contribute the same maximum activations. Local geometric relationships вҖ” the curvature of a surface patch, the orientation of an edge вҖ” are not captured. PointNet++ (Qi et al., 2017) adds hierarchical local feature extraction. Farthest Point Sampling (FPS) selects a subset of representative centroids by iteratively choosing the point furthest from all already-selected points вҖ” ensuring coverage without gaps. Ball query groups all points within a radius $r$ around each centroid into a local neighbourhood. A small PointNet processes each neighbourhood independently, producing a feature vector for each centroid. This grouping-and-abstracting is repeated hierarchically: local neighbourhoods at one scale become individual points at the next. Multi-Scale Grouping (MSG) applies ball query at multiple radii simultaneously and concatenates the resulting features, handling the density variability inherent in LiDAR point clouds (close objects are dense; far objects are sparse). PointNet++ achieves substantially better performance than PointNet on complex shapes and connects directly to the advanced architectures explored in chapter ida12.
overviews.ida10:
Chapters ida03вҖ“ida09 focus on discriminative models that map inputs to labels or structures. Generative models take the opposite perspective: learn the data distribution $p(x)$ itself. This enables synthesising new images, learning compact latent representations without labels, translating between visual domains, and detecting anomalies as regions of low probability. Chapter ida10 covers three progressively more powerful generative frameworks: autoencoders, variational autoencoders, and generative adversarial networks.

An autoencoder consists of an encoder $E: \mathcal{X} \to \mathcal{Z}$ mapping inputs to a lower-dimensional latent code, and a decoder $D: \mathcal{Z} \to \mathcal{X}$ mapping codes back to reconstructions. Training minimises the reconstruction loss $\mathcal{L} = \|x - D(E(x))\|^2$. The information bottleneck (the latent space is lower-dimensional than $x$) forces the encoder to compress meaningful structure; the decoder must recover the input from this compressed code. Autoencoders learn useful feature representations вҖ” the latent codes can serve as compact descriptors for retrieval or clustering вҖ” but the latent space has no probabilistic structure: there is no guarantee that a randomly sampled point in $\mathcal{Z}$ decodes to a realistic image. The latent manifold may have large gaps and discontinuities that produce nonsense when decoded.

The Variational Autoencoder (VAE, Kingma & Welling, 2013) adds probabilistic structure to the latent space. The encoder outputs parameters $\mu(x)$ and $\sigma^2(x)$ of a Gaussian posterior $q(z|x) = \mathcal{N}(z; \mu(x), \sigma^2(x) I)$; the decoder defines $p(x|z)$. Training maximises the Evidence Lower Bound (ELBO): $\mathcal{L}_{\text{ELBO}} = \mathbb{E}_{q(z|x)}[\log p(x|z)] - \text{KL}(q(z|x)\|p(z))$. The first term rewards faithful reconstruction; the second penalises deviation of the posterior from the prior $p(z) = \mathcal{N}(0,I)$, regularising the latent space. The reparameterisation trick $z = \mu(x) + \varepsilon \cdot \sigma(x)$, $\varepsilon \sim \mathcal{N}(0,I)$, makes the sampling step differentiable вҖ” the gradient flows through $\mu$ and $\sigma$ even though $z$ is stochastic. Forcing the posterior toward a standard Gaussian fills the latent space: interpolating between two codes $z_1$ and $z_2$ produces semantically smooth transitions through the data manifold. VAEs are the principled generative baseline from which diffusion models (ida11) are partly derived.

Generative Adversarial Networks (GANs, Goodfellow et al., 2014) train two networks in opposition. The generator $G: \mathcal{Z} \to \mathcal{X}$ maps random noise $z \sim p(z)$ to synthetic images. The discriminator $D: \mathcal{X} \to [0,1]$ predicts whether its input is real or generated. Training alternates between: updating $D$ to maximise $\mathbb{E}_{x\sim p_\text{data}}[\log D(x)] + \mathbb{E}_{z\sim p_z}[\log(1-D(G(z)))]$; and updating $G$ to maximise $\mathbb{E}_{z}[\log D(G(z))]$ (making $D$ believe generated images are real). At Nash equilibrium, $G$ produces images indistinguishable from real data; $D$ outputs $0.5$ everywhere. In practice, GAN training is notoriously unstable: mode collapse (the generator produces a few high-quality samples but ignores most of the data distribution), vanishing gradients (when $D$ is too confident), and oscillation rather than convergence. DCGAN adds convolutional architectures, batch normalisation, and specific initialisation to stabilise training. Progressive GAN grows resolution from $4\times4$ to $1024\times1024$ incrementally. Spectral normalisation constrains the discriminator's Lipschitz constant.

Conditional generation enables controlled synthesis. pix2pix (Isola et al., 2017) learns paired image-to-image translation (e.g., semantic map $\to$ photo) by conditioning both $G$ and $D$ on the input image, with a combined L1 reconstruction loss and adversarial loss вҖ” L1 reduces blurriness; adversarial loss enforces realism. CycleGAN (Zhu et al., 2017) extends this to unpaired training: learn $G: X\to Y$ and $F: Y\to X$ simultaneously with cycle consistency losses $\mathcal{L}_{\text{cyc}} = \mathbb{E}[\|G(F(y))-y\|_1] + \mathbb{E}[\|F(G(x))-x\|_1]$, constraining the two generators to be approximate inverses. This enables domain translation without paired examples вҖ” style transfer between artistic styles, horse-to-zebra, summer-to-winter вҖ” and connects to the broader area of domain adaptation. The GAN framework's adversarial training idea is replaced by the more stable diffusion process in chapter ida11.

Exercise baДҹlantДұsДұ вҖ” Exercise 10: Generative modellerin Keras/TensorFlow ile uygulamalДұ karЕҹДұlДұДҹДұ. VAE'nin MNIST Гјzerinde eДҹitimi ve latent space sГјrekliliДҹi analizi. GAN'Дұn minmax eДҹitimi, log(1-D(G(z))) gradyan sorununu max log(D(G(z)))'ye Г§evirme Г§Г¶zГјmГј. DCGAN, Conditional GAN (label'Дұ one-hot olarak generator/discriminator'a ekleme). CycleGAN teorisi: 2 generator + 2 discriminator, adversarial + cycle-consistency + identity loss ГјГ§lГјsГј.

Lab baДҹlantДұsДұ вҖ” Lab Session 6 (GAN): PyTorch ile vanilla GAN ve DCGAN implement etme. Discriminator ve Generator'Дұ FC layer'larla sДұfДұrdan yazma, BCE loss ile alternating training (discriminator step вҶ’ generator step). DCGAN'da Conv/ConvTranspose2d kullanДұmДұ, MNIST 64Г—64'e upsample edilerek eДҹitim. Training sДұrasДұnda Гјretilen gГ¶rГјntГјlerin adДұm adДұm nasДұl geliЕҹtiДҹini gГ¶zlemleme.
overviews.ida11:
GANs (ida10) generate high-quality images but are difficult to train and prone to mode collapse. Score-based generative models and diffusion models offer a principled probabilistic alternative: instead of learning to fool a discriminator, they learn to reverse a gradual noise corruption process. Diffusion models have become the dominant image synthesis paradigm, underlying Stable Diffusion, DALL-E 2, Imagen, and numerous scientific imaging applications.

The forward diffusion process adds Gaussian noise to data over $T$ steps, gradually destroying structure until the image is pure noise. Each step is defined by $q(x_t|x_{t-1}) = \mathcal{N}(x_t; \sqrt{1-\beta_t}\,x_{t-1}, \beta_t I)$ where $\beta_t$ is a small variance schedule (linearly increasing from $\beta_1 = 10^{-4}$ to $\beta_T = 0.02$ over $T=1000$ steps). The key algebraic property is that $x_t$ can be sampled directly from $x_0$ without iterating through all $t$ steps: $x_t = \sqrt{\bar{\alpha}_t}\,x_0 + \sqrt{1-\bar{\alpha}_t}\,\varepsilon$ where $\bar{\alpha}_t = \prod_{s=1}^t(1-\beta_s)$ and $\varepsilon \sim \mathcal{N}(0,I)$. At $t=T$, $\bar{\alpha}_T \approx 0$ and $x_T \approx \varepsilon$ вҖ” the image has been completely destroyed and replaced by Gaussian noise. This reparameterisation allows efficient training without sequential simulation.

The reverse process learns to denoise: given $x_t$, predict $x_{t-1}$. Because the true reverse conditional $q(x_{t-1}|x_t)$ depends on the entire data distribution, it is intractable. DDPM (Denoising Diffusion Probabilistic Models, Ho et al., 2020) parameterises the reverse step as $p_\theta(x_{t-1}|x_t) = \mathcal{N}(x_{t-1}; \mu_\theta(x_t, t), \Sigma_\theta(x_t, t))$ and trains a neural network $\varepsilon_\theta(x_t, t)$ to predict the noise $\varepsilon$ that was added in the forward step. The simplified training objective is $\mathcal{L} = \mathbb{E}_{t, x_0, \varepsilon}\left[\|\varepsilon - \varepsilon_\theta(\sqrt{\bar\alpha_t}x_0 + \sqrt{1-\bar\alpha_t}\varepsilon,\, t)\|^2\right]$ вҖ” just mean squared error between the true noise and the predicted noise. The architecture is a U-Net (ida05) with time-step conditioning (sinusoidal embeddings of $t$ injected at each layer via FiLM вҖ” Feature-wise Linear Modulation). At inference, $T=1000$ sequential denoising steps are required вҖ” substantially slower than GANs.

DDIM (Denoising Diffusion Implicit Models, Song et al., 2020) reformulates the reverse process as a non-Markovian deterministic trajectory using the same trained $\varepsilon_\theta$: $$x_{t-1} = \sqrt{\bar\alpha_{t-1}}\underbrace{\frac{x_t - \sqrt{1-\bar\alpha_t}\varepsilon_\theta}{\sqrt{\bar\alpha_t}}}_{\hat{x}_0\text{ estimate}} + \sqrt{1-\bar\alpha_{t-1}}\varepsilon_\theta.$$ Since the trajectory is deterministic, large steps can be skipped: 50 DDIM steps produce quality comparable to 1000 DDPM steps, a $20\times$ speedup. Classifier-free guidance (Ho & Salimans, 2022) enables conditional generation without a separate classifier. A single network $\varepsilon_\theta(x_t, t, c)$ is trained with conditioning $c$ (class label, text embedding) randomly dropped out with probability 10вҖ“20%, so the model learns both conditional and unconditional denoising. At inference, the guided prediction is $\hat\varepsilon = \varepsilon_\theta(x_t,t,\varnothing) + w\cdot(\varepsilon_\theta(x_t,t,c) - \varepsilon_\theta(x_t,t,\varnothing))$, where $w > 1$ is the guidance scale. High $w$ produces images that strongly match the condition but lack diversity; $w=1$ recovers the conditional model. Stable Diffusion (Rombach et al., 2022) applies the diffusion process not in pixel space but in the latent space of a pre-trained VAE (ida10), reducing computational cost by $8\times$ in each spatial dimension while preserving perceptual quality. Text conditioning is provided by CLIP text embeddings via cross-attention in the U-Net. The connection to score matching is exact: $\varepsilon_\theta \approx -\sqrt{1-\bar\alpha_t}\,\nabla_{x_t}\log q(x_t)$, so DDPM noise prediction is equivalent to estimating the score (gradient of log-density).

Exercise baДҹlantДұsДұ вҖ” Exercise 11: Diffusion modellerinin PyTorch (HuggingFace diffusers) ile uygulamasДұ. Д°lk kДұsДұmda MNIST Гјzerinde vanilla DDPM вҖ” UNet2DModel ile noise prediction, DDPMScheduler, backward denoising gГ¶rselleЕҹtirme. Д°kinci kДұsДұmda class-conditioned UNet: digit label'ДұnДұ embedding'e Г§evirip extra kanal olarak UNet input'una concatenate etme, inference'ta istenen rakamДұ Гјretme. Son kДұsДұmda Stable Diffusion pipeline'ДұnДұn parГ§alarДұ: VAE (512вҶ’64 latent), CLIP tokenizer + text encoder, cross-attention ile koЕҹullanmДұЕҹ UNet, guidance_scale ve num_inference_steps'in etkisi.

Lab baДҹlantДұsДұ вҖ” Lab Session 7 (Diffusion Model): DDPM'yi sДұfДұrdan implement etme вҖ” noise schedule (beta), forward corruption (Algorithm 1), backward denoising sampling (Algorithm 2), UNet2D denoising network. Sonra aynДұ Еҹeyi HuggingFace diffusers ile yaparak farkДұ gГ¶rme. CIFAR-10 Гјzerinde eДҹitim. Exercise 11'deki Stable Diffusion pipeline'Дұndan Г¶nce temeli kavramak iГ§in ideal.
overviews.ida12:
Chapter ida09 established PointNet and PointNet++ as the foundational architectures for point cloud processing. While PointNet++ adds local context through hierarchical grouping, it still relies on fixed neighbourhoods defined in Euclidean space. Chapter ida12 explores three architectures that address remaining limitations: robust density-adaptive convolution (FCPN), dynamic feature-space graph convolution (DGCNN), and shape generation through surface folding (FoldingNet).

Fully Convolutional Point Networks (FCPN, Rethage et al., 2018) tackle a practical limitation of both PointNet and PointNet++: sensitivity to non-uniform point density. LiDAR scans inherently produce denser point clouds for nearby surfaces and sparser ones for distant surfaces. Standard ball-query grouping (PointNet++) captures many points near the sensor and few far away, producing inconsistent local feature aggregation. FCPN replaces ball query with a sliding cylinder kernel: a vertical cylinder of fixed radius and height is placed at each centroid, and all points within it are aggregated. Cylinder grouping is invariant to the vertical distribution of points (important for outdoor 3D scenes where height is qualitatively different from lateral extent). Local convolution within the cylinder uses shared MLP features, similar to PointNet, but with the cylinder geometry providing a consistent spatial context. The fully convolutional design (no fixed-size global pooling) produces per-point or per-voxel predictions naturally, enabling dense semantic segmentation of large-scale outdoor scenes.

DGCNN (Dynamic Graph CNN, Wang et al., 2019) reformulates local feature aggregation as an operation on a dynamically constructed graph. For each point $x_i$, the $k$ nearest neighbours in the current feature space are found, and edge features are computed: $e_{ij} = h_\Theta(x_i,\, x_j - x_i)$ where $h_\Theta$ is a shared MLP applied to the concatenation of the central point feature and the relative neighbour feature (difference $x_j - x_i$ encodes local geometry). The EdgeConv operation aggregates over the $k$ edges: $x_i' = \max_{j\in\mathcal{N}(i)} e_{ij}$. The critical word is 'dynamic': the $k$-NN graph is recomputed in the updated feature space after each EdgeConv layer. In the first layer, neighbours are the $k$ nearest points in 3D Euclidean space (geometric proximity). In deeper layers, neighbours are found in learned feature space вҖ” two points on opposite ends of an elongated object may be far in Euclidean space but close in feature space (similar geometry), and DGCNN captures this non-local semantic relationship. This graph-rewiring across layers is the key distinction from PointNet++ (fixed Euclidean grouping). DGCNN achieves state-of-the-art results on both classification and part segmentation, with the dynamic graph naturally adapting to the task.

FoldingNet (Yang et al., 2018) addresses point cloud generation and reconstruction вҖ” the inverse problem of analysis. Given a point cloud, encode it into a compact codeword; given a codeword, decode a point cloud that approximates the original shape. The encoder is a PointNet-like architecture producing a fixed-length codeword $\mathbf{c} \in \mathbb{R}^{512}$. The decoder is the novel contribution: it takes a fixed 2D grid of points (parameterising a flat surface) and deforms it into a 3D surface approximating the input shape through two sequential folding operations.
